The scope summary captures the first paragraph of the scope's Intent section
Missing or malformed scope files result in no scope summary, not an error
An UNKNOWN result with an existing verify report triggers a warning
The dashboard shows total runs and counts for each finding status
The dashboard highlights files that generated findings across multiple pipeline runs
The dashboard groups active findings by file
The dashboard caps displayed active findings at 30
Work completion output shows active finding count instead of total callouts
A maintenance summary appears when findings were auto-closed or classified
Existing proof chain entries have their callouts field migrated to findings

entries.scope_summary: The proof chain records intelligence but can't act on it. Verify produces findings every pipeline run. Those findings sit in a flat list with no lifecycle state — no way to distinguish "needs attention" from "already handled," no way to mark stale observations, no way to classify informational upstream findings separately from actionable code/test findings. Agents consuming `ana proof context` receive 82 undifferentiated findings including observations about deleted files, absent code anchors, and superseded older observations alongside genuine active findings.

findings.summary: Dead ternary on new finding status: `packages/cli/src/commands/work.ts:810` — `(c as { category: string }).category === 'upstream' ? 'active' : 'active' as const` — both branches evaluate to `'active'`, making the expression a no-op. The correct assignment happens at lines 818-824. This is dead logic that should either be removed (let lines 818-824 handle it alone) or corrected to `'lesson' : 'active'` and the redundant loop removed.
findings.closed_reason: code changed, anchor absent

findings.summary: Redundant status filter in Hot Modules: `packages/cli/src/utils/proofSummary.ts:535-536` — double-checks `finding.status` both as truthy and not-undefined, then re-checks on the next line. A single `if (finding.status !== 'active' && finding.status !== undefined) continue;` would be clearer.
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in pre-F2 cleanup (39df6ba). Redundant double-filter simplified to single conditional. Anchor too generic for mechanical detection.

findings.summary: Dashboard duplicates Active Issues logic: `packages/cli/src/utils/proofSummary.ts:566-616` — reimplements the collection, filtering, capping, and file-grouping from `generateActiveIssuesMarkdown` (lines 385-473). The format differs (### vs ## headings, no truncation), but extracting shared helpers for the filtering and grouping would reduce the ~50 lines of duplication.
findings.closed_reason: code changed, anchor absent

findings.summary: A015/A016 edge cases not behaviorally exercised: `packages/cli/tests/commands/work.test.ts:1278` — the supersession test proves the core mechanism but doesn't include an unresolved-basename finding (to prove A015's skip) or two same-entry findings with matching file+category (to prove A016's guard). The code guards are trivial and correct by inspection, but the test coverage gap means a regression in those guards wouldn't be caught.
findings.closed_reason: Stale — A015/A016 coverage gap filled by dedicated test at work.test.ts:1385 ('closes findings for deleted files'). Original supersession test at ~1278 still lacks these edges, but the guards are now exercised by the newer test.

findings.summary: A024 warning test doesn't trigger the warning: `packages/cli/tests/commands/work.test.ts:1372` — the test is tagged `@ana A024` but only asserts the entry has `result === 'PASS'`. The UNKNOWN warning path is unreachable through `completeWork` because of pre-validation. A direct `writeProofChain` test with an UNKNOWN-result proof object would exercise the actual warning.
findings.closed_reason: code changed, anchor absent

findings.summary: Backward compat risk on callouts migration: `packages/cli/src/commands/work.ts:841` — `delete existingAny['callouts']` removes the old field during migration. If an older CLI version reads the migrated `proof_chain.json`, it will see no callouts on those entries. Since the CLI is installed per-project, this is low risk, but the one-way migration means you can't roll back after the first `work complete`.
findings.closed_reason: upstream

build_concerns.summary: A024 UNKNOWN warning untestable through completeWork: The UNKNOWN result warning in `writeProofChain` cannot fire through `completeWork` because `completeWork` validates the verify report for PASS/FAIL before calling `writeProofChain`. The warning code is correct and will fire if `writeProofChain` is called directly with an UNKNOWN result, but the integration test cannot trigger it through the public `completeWork` API.
Pre-existing lint warnings: 14 warnings in 4 test files (analyzer-contract, confirmation, imports, ai-sdk-detection) for `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any`. Not introduced by this build.

Supersession test creates files on disk: The supersession test writes `src/shared.ts` to prevent the file-deleted staleness check from closing findings before supersession can operate. This is correct behavior but adds filesystem state to what could be a unit test. Verified complete by second pass.

entries.feature: Fix artifact save bypass, cwd bug, and work complete crash recovery

Work complete blocks when verify-report was never saved through the pipeline

Work complete blocks when both reports are missing and names the verify-report too

entries.scope_summary: Three interrelated bugs surfaced during the findings-lifecycle-foundation completion (2026-04-27). Build manually committed `build_report.md` via `git add && git commit`, bypassing `ana artifact save`. Save reported "already up to date" and skipped its entire metadata pipeline. Then `work complete` ran from `packages/cli/` (a subdirectory), causing `git add .ana/plans/` to fail on a relative path. `process.exit(1)` fired after the plan directory was already moved and proof chain already written. A second `work complete` said "already completed" because `active/` was empty.

findings.summary: A020 uses source-code reading instead of behavioral test: `packages/cli/tests/commands/work.test.ts:1736` — reads `work.ts` source and asserts the retry string exists. This proves the string is in the code but not that it appears in the error output when a commit actually fails. A behavioral test would mock `execSync` to throw on `git commit` and capture stderr. Low risk — the error path is straightforward (`catch` → `console.error` → `process.exit(1)`), but a source-reading test survives refactoring that breaks the behavior.
findings.closed_reason: promoted to testing-standards Rules: never assert on source code content

findings.summary: Dead ternary on finding status: `packages/cli/src/commands/work.ts:810` — `(c as { category: string }).category === 'upstream' ? 'active' : 'active' as const` — both branches evaluate to `'active'`. This is a pre-existing issue (from proof chain history) and is immediately overwritten by the status assignment loop at lines 818-824. Not introduced by this build; dormant dead code.
findings.closed_reason: code changed, anchor absent

findings.summary: Recovery catch swallows git status failure: `packages/cli/src/commands/work.ts:1080` — if `git status --porcelain .ana/` throws (e.g., corrupt `.git` directory), the catch silently falls through to the "already completed" message. This is unlikely but means a corrupted git state would report "already completed" instead of a diagnostic error. The spec doesn't cover this edge case, so it's not a FAIL — but it's a sharp edge.
findings.closed_reason: resolved: harden-hot-files surfaced git failures in catch (9ac6644)

findings.summary: A019 double recovery simulates failure via untracked file, not real commit failure: `packages/cli/tests/commands/work.test.ts:1714-1720` — the "second failure" is simulated by writing a new file to the completed directory, making `git status --porcelain .ana/` return non-empty. This is a valid test of the recovery detection path, but it doesn't prove the system recovers from two sequential real commit failures. Given the recovery code is identical to the initial commit path, this is acceptable.
findings.closed_reason: code changed, anchor absent

findings.summary: Contract A005 tests effect rather than literal return value: Contract specifies `target: "writeResult"` / `matcher: "equals"` / `value: false`, implying the test should capture and assert the return value of `writeSaveMetadata`. The test instead verifies the consequence (hash unchanged after idempotent skip). The verification is equivalent in practice — if `writeSaveMetadata` returned `true`, it would write a new timestamp, and the hash comparison would still pass but `saved_at` would change. The A006 test covers the timestamp preservation separately. Consider aligning the contract target with the actual test approach on next seal.
findings.closed_reason: upstream

assertions.says: A finding referencing a file by partial monorepo path is not closed when the file exists elsewhere in the project
A partial monorepo path finding stays active after staleness checks

Both old and new findings on the same file stay active

Reopened findings have their closure metadata cleared

Files with slashes that do not exist at declared path enter resolution
Bare basenames still enter the resolution chain
Anchor checks run on files verified to exist and close when anchor is absent
Anchor checks do not run on files that do not exist at the declared path
The verify template uses repo-relative paths in its example
The verify template includes guidance to reference existing findings
The template and dogfood copies are identical

entries.scope_summary: The proof chain's mechanical maintenance layer shipped with 57% closure accuracy. 16 of 37 closures destroyed valid observations. Three bugs: path resolution closed 4 files that exist at different monorepo paths, staleness checks overwrote 3 upstream lessons, and supersession destroyed 9 independent observations using a heuristic too coarse to distinguish same-issue from different-issue-on-same-file. Additionally, 6 findings with bare basenames (no `/`) escape staleness entirely because the syntactic gate skips them — 4 of these reference genuinely deleted files (`projectKind.ts` renamed to `applicationShape.ts` months ago).

findings.summary: Dead truthiness guard after parameter change: `packages/cli/src/utils/proofSummary.ts:346` — `else if (projectRoot)` was meaningful when `projectRoot` was optional; now it's always truthy since the parameter is required `string`. Remove the guard and outdent the glob fallback block.
findings.closed_reason: code changed, anchor absent

findings.summary: `delete` instead of explicit `undefined` in reopen loop: `packages/cli/src/commands/work.ts:887-889` — Spec says "Don't use `delete` — set explicitly so the JSON serialization is clean." Builder used `delete`. Functionally identical for `JSON.stringify` output (both omit the property), but deviates from spec guidance. Not a blocker — the behavior is correct.
findings.closed_reason: Accepted: delete and explicit undefined produce identical behavior for optional properties. Same JSON output, same type safety. Not worth changing.

findings.summary: Dead ternary on finding status: `packages/cli/src/commands/work.ts:811` ��� `category === 'upstream' ? 'active' : 'active'` — both branches identical. Pre-existing issue, not introduced by this build. Still present — see proof chain finding from "Fix artifact save bypass, cwd bug, and work complete crash recovery."
findings.closed_reason: code changed, anchor absent

findings.summary: A018/A019/A020 tag collision with other contracts: The pre-check reports these as COVERED, but the matched tags belong to tests from other features (readme.test.ts, confirmation.test.ts, scanProject.test.ts, proof.test.ts). No NEW tagged tests were written for this contract's template assertions. The existing `agent-proof-context.test.ts:66-75` (`@ana A008`) does verify template-dogfood sync byte-for-byte, and the template content was verified directly. Functional coverage exists; formal tag coverage for this contract does not.
findings.closed_reason: Root cause fixed by diff-scoped-tag-search (entry #27). Pre-check now searches added lines only.

findings.summary: A011 assertion checks one value not cleared state: `packages/cli/tests/commands/work.test.ts:1447` — asserts `closed_reason` is not `'superseded by new-C1'` but doesn't assert `closed_at` or `closed_by` are also cleared. The test proves the specific contract assertion (matcher: `not_equals`, value: `'superseded by new-C1'`) but a stronger test would verify all three closure fields are absent.
findings.closed_reason: Fixed — work.test.ts:1444-1445 now asserts both not.toBe('closed') AND toBe('active'). Exactly what the finding requested.

findings.summary: Contract A011 matcher is weak: The contract specifies `not_equals` / `"superseded by new-C1"` which only proves one field changed. A stronger contract assertion would check `closed_reason` is `undefined`/absent, confirming full metadata clearance rather than just "not the old value."
findings.closed_reason: upstream

build_concerns.summary: `delete` vs `undefined` for reopen cleanup: Spec explicitly says "Don't use `delete` — set explicitly so the JSON serialization is clean." Implementation uses `delete` because `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` prevents `undefined` assignment. Both approaches produce identical JSON output (property omitted). Deviation is mechanical, not semantic.

Glob performance on large projects. The `globResultCache` prevents repeated glob calls for the same basename, but the first glob for each unique basename traverses the filesystem. For projects with many findings referencing different basenames, this could add latency. The existing `resolveFindingPaths` already does glob calls, so this is consistent with existing performance characteristics. Verified complete by second pass.

entries.scope_summary: The proof chain's highest-value data — findings and build concerns — enters through 216 lines of regex parsing free-text LLM markdown. Every other data type (assertions, timing, hashes, modules) enters through structured sources at 100% reliability. The parser is adequate for what it captures (89% file resolution, 92% anchor accuracy post-F1.5). But two fields that would make the learning loop genuinely intelligent — `related_assertions` (which contract assertion a finding relates to) and `severity` (blocker vs observation vs note) — can't be parser-extracted. The agent knows which assertion it was checking when it found the issue. It just has no structured place to write it.

findings.summary: Double YAML parse in companion success message — validateVerifyDataFormat/validateBuildDataFormat parses the file, then saveArtifact re-parses at lines 932-933 to count findings for the console message. Validation function could return the parsed count.
findings.closed_reason: Acceptable inefficiency: double YAML parse at artifact.ts:1353-1354 re-reads companion file (<1KB) to count findings for a success message. The file was already parsed by validateVerifyDataFormat/validateBuildDataFormat. But YAML parse of a <1KB file takes microseconds. Same reasoning as getMainTreeRoot duplication (closed this session) — cheap operation, not worth refactoring the validate return type to thread a count through.

findings.summary: blocks-save tests (A006, A007) use toThrow() without checking exit code or error message content. saveArtifact calls process.exit(1), which throws in test — the assertion confirms the throw but not the exit code value or the descriptive error message.
findings.closed_reason: Adequate: bare toThrow() at artifact.test.ts:384 catches process.exit(1) from branch enforcement (line 1110). Steps 0-4 before the branch check are lightweight config reads (parse type, resolve root, read ana.json, get branch) that pass with the test fixture. A false positive would require a broken fixture — a test bug, not an assertion weakness. The fixture at lines 380-382 is specifically designed to trigger the branch rejection for build-report on artifact branch.

findings.summary: Contract IDs A022-A037 collide with pre-existing @ana tags from other features' contracts. Pre-check cannot distinguish — it reports COVERED for any matching tag regardless of feature. This makes pre-check unreliable for projects with 10+ pipeline cycles.

findings.summary: Cache never invalidated — stale if files created between resolveFindingPaths calls within one writeProofChain invocation
findings.closed_reason: Unreachable: resolveFindingPaths is called 4 times in writeProofChain (work.ts:940-949) sharing one globCache. All calls are synchronous with no I/O between them — no files can be created between calls. The cache lives for one writeProofChain invocation and is discarded. Cache staleness requires file creation between sync calls, which can't happen in a single-threaded Node.js process.

entries.scope_summary: Two fixes on the same files. First: git commit messages are constructed via template literals and passed through the shell — user-controlled text in `--reason` can break commits or theoretically execute code. Replace all 5 `execSync` git commit calls with `spawnSync` so message content is never shell-interpreted. Second: the `work complete` output has a nudge block that re-reads the entire proof_chain.json to decide whether to show a recommendation. The nudge is the wrong design — it's a one-shot discoverability hack with an arbitrary threshold, not a data-driven signal. Remove it entirely and replace the chain output line with a cleaner format that includes the per-run finding delta.

findings.summary: Severity migration does not handle unexpected old values beyond blocker/note — if a future writer introduces an unknown severity value (e.g. from a malformed manual edit), the migration loop silently ignores it. entries.scope_summary: The developer wants to see quality trajectory across pipeline runs. `ana proof health` computes severity-weighted metrics, identifies hot modules, surfaces promotion candidates, and reports on promotion effectiveness. The `work complete` output gains a change-driven fourth line that surfaces health insights when something shifted since the last run. Health is the measurement layer that validates the severity taxonomy, informs promotion decisions, and provides the north star metric (risks/run trending down).

findings.summary: Hardcoded 10 in trend display instead of using MIN_ENTRIES_FOR_TREND constant. Template literal uses ${10} rather than importing and using the named constant, creating drift risk if threshold changes.
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in health-display-polish — line 276 now uses MIN_ENTRIES_FOR_TREND constant instead of hardcoded 10.

findings.summary: detectHealthChange 'detects trend improvement' unit test uses conditional assertion (if change.changed) — if change.changed is false, the expect on triggers never executes. This masks a potential false pass.
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-type-lies — if (change.changed) guard removed from detectHealthChange test. Assertion now fires unconditionally.

findings.summary: Promotion candidate display has no summary truncation. Long summaries from findings (up to 1000 chars) render untruncated in terminal output. Not a crash risk but degrades terminal readability.
findings.closed_reason: Fixed — health display now truncates promotion candidate summaries at 100 chars (proof.ts:353-355, 369-371).

findings.summary: Promotion effectiveness test covers only the extremes (0% reduction, 100% reduction, tracking). No test for intermediate reduction (e.g., 40%) or negative reduction (more matches than baseline).
findings.closed_reason: Covered: promotion effectiveness status is binary — reductionPct > 0 = 'effective', ≤ 0 = 'ineffective' (proofSummary.ts:903). Tests cover both branches: A024 tests 100% reduction (effective), A037 tests 0% reduction (ineffective), A023 tests tracking (< 5 entries). The reduction formula is Math.round((1 - matches/total) * 100) — simple arithmetic where testing extremes validates the formula. An intermediate test (e.g., 40%) would exercise the same code path as A024.

findings.summary: Trajectory 'worsening' label can be counterintuitive with sparse classification — reports worsening on 0.1 risks/run when most findings lack severity. Algorithm is correct but label may mislead operators.
findings.closed_reason: Data transition artifact, not algorithm bug: trajectory shows 'worsening' because entries 1-21 are partially/fully unclassified (pre-enrichment schema) → risk counts artificially 0 → first-half average is near-zero. The algorithm at proofSummary.ts:711 correctly excludes fully-unclassified entries but includes partially-classified ones with 0 risk. New customers start fully classified — they won't see this. Our dogfood data has the transition artifact. Connected to kind backfill future idea in notes.

entries.scope_summary: The proof system computes quality intelligence — severity classifications, suggested actions, promotion candidates, trend trajectories. The CLI commands that surface this data (`ana proof audit`, `work complete`) show the data but don't tell developers what to do with it. "71 active findings across 23 files" is a number. "5 risk, 4 promote, 9 closeable" is a decision surface. "Health: trend worsened" is a fact. "Health: trend worsened → ana proof audit" is a nudge. Two display additions (~25 lines of production code) that transform proof output from data dumps into action surfaces.

entries.scope_summary: Pre-check's tag coverage check is a broken trust layer that corrupts the proof chain. It greps test files for `@ana` tags to report COVERED/UNCOVERED per assertion — but after 33 builds, tag collision makes the signal unreliable (42% of entries have collision findings), and its COVERED/UNCOVERED output infiltrates the permanent record where Verify's independent judgment should be the authority. Two diseases, one root cause. The fix: remove the mechanism entirely, make Verify the sole source of assertion status, and let pre-check do the one job it does well — seal verification.

findings.summary: `as 'PASS' | 'FAIL'` cast in parseResult relies on regex constraint, not type-level proof — safe but brittle if regex changes
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: parseResult at proofSummary.ts:197-199 is a 3-line function where the regex, toUpperCase, and type cast are on the same line. The regex constrains the match to PASS|FAIL, toUpperCase normalizes, and the cast types the result. Modifying the regex to accept new values without updating the cast would require editing the same line — the proximity makes the coupling visible and safe.

findings.summary: fileMatches `includes('/')` treats `./census.ts` as directory-qualified — theoretical false negative for dot-slash prefixed paths
findings.closed_reason: Real but unreachable in practice: fileMatches at proofSummary.ts:2090 does produce a false negative for './census.ts' queries — both paths include '/' so the suffix check runs and fails. However, no system-produced path uses './' prefix — contract file_changes, scan engine, and proof context all use normalized paths without './' . The edge case requires manual 'ana proof context ./foo.ts' input, where the user would retry without './'.

A template gives the wrong filename for multi-phase builds, causing save failures. A type says `skill: string` but Commander makes it optional at runtime. When dogfood files go out of sync, it's typically a bulk template update — all files are fixed at once, not iteratively. The masking concern (not seeing later failures) doesn't match the actual fix workflow. test.each() would be marginally better for diagnostics but doesn't change the fix action.

entries.scope_summary: The health dashboard is the proof system's face. It's what a founder screenshots for their co-founder, what a developer checks before scoping work, what Learn reads at startup. Three gaps: the stats don't tell the full story (no verification effectiveness, no pipeline velocity), the timing data lies (think and plan show identical values), and nothing stops failed work from entering the proof chain. Three phases, one goal: the dashboard tells the truth and the proof chain guards its own integrity.

findings.summary: A014 cap test uses toBeLessThanOrEqual(5) instead of toBe(5) — passes even if cap logic is broken and returns 0 items
findings.closed_reason: Covered by combined assertions: toContain('Next Actions') at proof.test.ts:2778 proves ≥1 item (section only renders when cappedActions.length > 0, proof.ts:564). toBeLessThanOrEqual(5) at line 2781 proves ≤5 items. Together: 1-5 items from 8 candidates. The cap is slice(0, MAX_NEXT_ACTIONS) where MAX_NEXT_ACTIONS=5 — produces exactly 5 from any input ≥5. With 8 promote candidates all matching the filter, the output is deterministically 5.

findings.summary: computeFirstPassRate exported but never imported outside proofSummary.ts — only called internally by computeHealthReport

findings.closed_reason: Covered through integration: computeFirstPassRate is tested at proof.test.ts:2834 with exact assertion toBe(80) — 4 of 5 entries with 0 rejection cycles. computePipelineStats is tested at line 2836-2837 with structural and value assertions. Both functions are simple (counter + division for first-pass, median for pipeline). Integration path through runProof(['health', '--json']) exercises the full computation. Direct unit tests would duplicate the integration assertions.

entries.scope_summary: Learn triage (2026-05-02) reviewed 62 active findings across 41 pipeline runs and identified 10 that share one disease: tests that pass without proving anything. Weak matchers (`toBeDefined()`, `toBeGreaterThan(0)`), conditional guards that make assertions vacuous, and `not.toContain()` that passes by coincidence of fixture data. An 11th finding (risk severity) surfaces zero coverage for the UNVERIFIED fallback path — a safety-critical code path where silent failure corrupts the proof chain.

findings.summary: A014 nudge check uses specific patterns ('→ claude', '→ ana proof') — a new nudge format would slip through
findings.closed_reason: Tests are correctly scoped: each test at work.test.ts:2912/2955/3005/3058 verifies a specific trigger→nudge mapping (new_candidates→learn, trend_worsened→audit, informational→no nudge). The hardcoded patterns ('→ claude', '→ ana proof') are the ACTUAL nudge text — asserting on them is asserting on the correct output. If a new nudge type is added, a new test would be written for it. The tests verify what exists, not what might exist in the future.

findings.summary: Remaining toBeGreaterThan(0) in proofSummary.test.ts — 21 instances outside this spec's scope still use weak assertions
findings.closed_reason: Largely resolved: was 21 instances, now 5. The remaining 5 toBeGreaterThan(0) in proofSummary.test.ts are 'non-empty result' assertions — hot_modules.length (2), scopeCandidates.length (1), total_stale (2). These verify the fixture produces output, not a specific count. toBeGreaterThan(0) IS the correct assertion for 'this list should not be empty given this fixture.' Asserting exact counts would couple to fixture internals.

findings.summary: Timestamp recency check (before/after window) in A010 test may flake on extremely slow CI — window depends on test execution speed
findings.closed_reason: Finding's concern is inverted: the before/after timestamp window test at work.test.ts:3453-3465 CANNOT flake from slow CI. Slow execution widens the window (before is earlier, after is later), making the assertion easier to satisfy, not harder. The only flake scenario would be a clock jump backward, which is a system issue not a test issue.

entries.scope_summary: Restructure Learn's triage workflow to eliminate the batch-closing failure mode caused by Phase 1 "Accept-Action (Clear the Deck)." Three consecutive sessions proved that grouping findings by `suggested_action: accept` creates batch thinking — Learn recommends closing the entire group without individual evaluation, regardless of template instructions. The fix removes the action-based grouping and triages by severity instead, extending the rigorous Phase 2 workflow to ALL findings.

findings.summary: prepublishOnly relies on relative ../../ path — breaks if package depth changes
findings.closed_reason: Monorepo structural invariant: packages/cli at depth 2 is the product's fixed location. ../../ at package.json:53 matches this depth. Moving the package would break imports, CI, release scripts, workspace config, and test paths — ../../ is the least fragile thing that would break. The monorepo layout is a structural constant, not a variable.

findings.summary: README.md and CHANGELOG.md cannot be verified with npm pack --dry-run — only exist after prepublishOnly
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: README.md and CHANGELOG.md are copied from monorepo root by prepublishOnly (package.json:53). They don't exist in packages/cli/ during development. This is the standard monorepo publish pattern — npm pack --dry-run can't verify them, and that's expected.

findings.summary: documentation.test.ts assertion removed for packages/cli/README.md — justified but reduces dogfood coverage
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: packages/cli/README.md doesn't exist in git — it's copied from root by prepublishOnly (package.json:53). The test correctly doesn't assert its existence. Comment at documentation.test.ts:276 explains: 'README.md is a prepublishOnly artifact, not tracked in git.'

This is the third of three V1 scopes — docs and code changes must be merged first. After this scope ships and merges, the publish ceremony is: tag, push, verify.

findings.summary: npm pack dry-run doesn't include README.md or CHANGELOG.md — prepublishOnly required first
findings.closed_reason: Duplicate: identical concern to v1-documentation-overhaul-C4 (closed this session). Standard monorepo publish pattern.

findings.summary: Release script 'cd packages/cli && npm version' requires a semver argument — no guard or help text
findings.closed_reason: Acceptable: release script at package.json:12 without args prints npm version dump (exit 0) — confusing but immediately recognizable as 'forgot the arg.' Single-user workflow where the operator knows the tool. Adding a shell guard is non-trivial due to pnpm arg passing mechanics (pnpm appends args to the command string, doesn't set $1). Risk of breaking the release workflow outweighs benefit of preventing 10 seconds of confusion.

findings.summary: release.yml copies README/CHANGELOG separately from prepublishOnly — two sources of truth for doc copying
findings.closed_reason: Documented redundancy: release.yml:31 comment says '(same as prepublishOnly)' — the CI copy at line 32 is explicitly acknowledged as redundant with prepublishOnly at package.json:53. Both do the same cp command. npm publish triggers prepublishOnly which is authoritative. The CI copy is belt-and-suspenders, and the comment coordinates maintenance if either changes.

findings.summary: No dedicated tests for v1-release-prep contract — assertions verified by source inspection only
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: v1-release-prep was a one-time release configuration scope (version bumps, package.json metadata, publish config). entries.scope_summary: Eliminate the command injection attack surface across the CLI. The product targets a clone-and-run deployment — developers clone repos containing `.ana/ana.json` and run pipeline commands. A malicious config file can currently execute arbitrary shell commands because user-controlled and config-controlled values reach `execSync` via string interpolation without validation. This must be mechanically impossible before seeking users.

findings.summary: A016-A019 @ana tags point to pre-existing branchPrefix template tests, not command entry point validation
findings.closed_reason: Systemic tag collision: A016-A019 IDs reused across scopes (lesson C3). Tags point to correct tests for their respective scopes (kind-aware-branch-prefixes). Fixing for security-hardening would be overwritten by the next scope using these IDs. The security hardening entry points have validateSlug wired — artifact.ts:1124/1570, work.ts:1071, pr.ts:154, proof.ts:1662 — verified by code inspection.

findings.summary: No dedicated integration tests for command entry point injection rejection — saveArtifact, completeWork, createPr, strengthen
findings.closed_reason: Security wiring verified: validateSlug is the first call in completeWork (work.ts:1071), saveArtifact (artifact.ts:1124/1570), createPr (pr.ts:154). validateSkillName at proof.ts:1662 for strengthen. All validators unit tested at validators.test.ts:21-32 with injection payloads. Wiring is one line deep with no conditional logic between entry point and validator.

findings.closed_reason: Stale: getCurrentBranch at git-operations.ts:208 uses runGit (hardened spawnSync wrapper with shell:false, array args), NOT execSync. The function was migrated from execSync to runGit by the kind-aware-branch-prefixes scope after the security-hardening scope noted it. The finding's claim is false for the current code.

findings.summary: SLUG_PATTERN exported but only consumed by test file — no source imports the raw regex
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: SLUG_PATTERN exported at validators.ts:19, used internally at line 43 (validateSlug), imported by validators.test.ts for direct regex validation. Exporting constants for test access is standard — same pattern as branchExists and formatHumanReadable (closed this session).

findings.summary: A010 test mocks process.exit — after mock, readArtifactBranch continues and returns invalid branch to caller. Correct in production but test pattern allows post-exit execution.
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: process.exit mock at git-operations.test.ts:237 is the standard Vitest pattern for testing exit behavior. Post-exit continuation is a mock artifact — in production, process.exit(1) terminates. Test correctly asserts exitCode === 1 and error message contains 'Invalid artifactBranch.'

findings.summary: Enforcement test (A023) asserts on source code content via grep — violates testing-standards skill rule 'never assert on source code content' but is the only practical way to enforce convention. Spec explicitly requested this pattern.
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A023 enforcement test at git-operations.test.ts:463-485 greps source for execSync usage. Source-code assertion is the only way to enforce a security invariant ('no execSync in production code'). There is no behavioral alternative — the test ensures nobody adds execSync, which can only be verified by inspection.

findings.summary: git.ts in src/engine/detectors/ retains execSync — architecturally correct (engine boundary, not commands/utils) but is the last remaining execSync in the codebase outside tests. Future hardening could migrate this to spawnSync for consistency.

Enforcement test comment-filter heuristic checks line prefix only (starts with //, *, /*). An execSync buried mid-line after non-comment code wouldn't be caught if the line also starts with a comment-like pattern. Low probability given codebase conventions.
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: comment-filter at git-operations.test.ts:478-481 checks line prefix (starts with //, *, /*). Edge case — execSync in a multi-line comment body after * — would be a false positive (flagging a comment as usage), making the test MORE strict, not less. Sufficient for the security invariant.

findings.summary: runGit defaults exitCode to 1 when spawnSync returns null status (signal kill). This is reasonable but means SIGKILL'd git processes appear as generic failures — no way to distinguish 'command failed' from 'process was killed'. Acceptable for CLI use.
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: runGit at git-operations.ts:47 defaults exitCode to 1 when spawnSync returns status:null (signal kill or spawn failure). This is the safest behavior — treats exceptional conditions (OOM kill, git not found) as failures, letting callers handle through existing error paths. The alternative (throwing or special exit code) would crash commands instead of degrading gracefully. Finding says 'reasonable.'

entries.scope_summary: Consolidation pass on the proof intelligence surface. Two confirmed bugs corrupt data, the staleness detector produces 78% false positives, shared logic is copy-pasted three times, and the audit display conflates actionable findings with monitoring noise. These aren't independent problems — they grew feature-by-feature without a consolidation pass. The proof surface now undermines its own credibility: the tool that verifies AI work can't get its own verification data right.

entries.scope_summary: Fix the infrastructure that makes Learn sessions slow, error-prone, and wasteful. Six changes that each address a verified problem from 3 Learn sessions. The proof commands have push error handling that confuses Learn, a pre-commit hook that wastes 30s per close on JSON files, severity counts that lie, duplicated search loops, an audit command without filters, and a template missing critical guidance.

findings.summary: A001 test verifies retry success path, not the failure message — contract matcher/value ('Push failed after retry') never asserted
findings.closed_reason: Covered: commitAndPushProofChanges is a private shared helper (proof.ts:156) tested through the close command's A001 push retry test. The retry failure message at lines 184/188/195 is a static string ('Push failed after retry — run git push') — identical across all 3 failure conditions. Testing the failure path requires a second conflicting commit during the test, which is disproportionate to the value of asserting a static string.

findings.summary: A018 uses toBeGreaterThan(0) — weak assertion; the fixture has exactly 1 unclassified finding, test should use toBe(1)
pullBeforeRead calls process.exit(1) on rebase conflict without running git rebase --abort first — leaves dirty rebase state
findings.closed_reason: Stale: rebase --abort added at proof.ts:133 — the conflict path now runs runGit(['rebase', '--abort']) before process.exit(1). Fixed by a subsequent commit after the proof-system-near-term scope.

findings.summary: Audit severity filter uses reverse-index splice loop — O(n²) on large finding sets; Array.filter() would be clearer and faster
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in 0eea04a7 — replaced two reverse-index splice loops with Array.filter() in proof.ts audit command. Changed const activeFindings to let. Same predicates, same order, same result — O(n) instead of O(n²). All 3 filter tests pass (--severity, --entry, --severity+json).

findings.summary: Pre-commit filter also skips for .claude/-only commits — broader than contract A006 specifies (only mentions .ana/). Pragmatic but unspecified.
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: pre-commit filter at .husky/pre-commit:36 skips .claude/ and website/ in addition to .ana/. Both are correct — .claude/ contains markdown agent/skill files (no TypeScript), and the pre-commit only runs packages/cli checks (lines 42-46) which website changes can't affect. Pragmatic extension beyond contract A006.

findings.summary: No dedicated push retry tests for lesson, promote, or strengthen — shared helper covers them but no integration test proves the wiring
findings.closed_reason: Covered: commitAndPushProofChanges at proof.ts:156 is call-site independent — takes (root, files, message, coAuthor) and doesn't branch on which command invoked it. Called by close (line 932), lesson (1200), promote (1509), strengthen (1801) with identical structure. Testing through close's A001 test IS testing the function for all callers. Adding 3 duplicate retry tests for lesson/promote/strengthen would test the same code path.

findings.summary: No test coverage for pre-commit bypass behavior (A006/A007) — shell hook not exercised in vitest suite
findings.closed_reason: Structural limitation: pre-commit hook is a shell script (.husky/pre-commit:32-40). Vitest runs Node.js tests — exercising shell hooks requires a separate integration test infrastructure (create repos, stage files, invoke hook, verify outcomes). The 8-line shell logic is stable and CI catches regressions if the skip filter is too broad.

findings.summary: Proof context finding 'Lesson command catch block at proof.ts:1141 loses error detail' — this build replaces that catch block with commitAndPushProofChanges which includes stderr. Stale finding likely resolved.
findings.closed_reason: upstream

entries.scope_summary: Make the first 10 minutes of using Anatomia feel professional. A developer who runs `ana --help` should immediately understand what the tool does, which commands matter, and how to start. Right now: flat command list, jargon descriptions, no examples, wrong ordering, misleading CTA. Every competitor (gh, npm, docker) has grouped commands, clear descriptions, and examples. We don't.

findings.summary: agents.ts file header comment still says 'List deployed agents' — stale after description change
findings.closed_reason: Stale — 'List deployed agents' no longer exists in agents.ts. File header (line 2) now reads 'Agent dashboard and model management.' The comment was corrected by a subsequent pipeline run.

findings.summary: addHelpCommand(false) breaks 'ana help <cmd>' — users who learned that pattern get an error
findings.closed_reason: Scoped as future work: ana help <cmd> is disabled (addHelpCommand(false) at index.ts:36) to keep grouped --help output clean. ana --help and ana <cmd> --help both work. The help subcommand pattern is standard in CLIs (git, gh, docker) and should be re-enabled with proper UX research. Scope prompt prepared for Ana Think to research CLI help conventions, Commander API options, and implement cleanly.

entries.scope_summary: The pipeline shares a single working directory and git index across all agents. The branch dance — switching, stashing, validating, recovering — is template-enforced, not mechanically enforced. 30+ lines of branch management instructions tell agents what to do. Nothing prevents them from doing it wrong. This produced a real data corruption incident (ANA-CLI-034: two Plan agents shared a git index, one committed the other's staged files) and creates daily friction (stashing, blocking, wrong-branch errors).

Scope covers 3 hard-block guards with lightest possible fixtures. Finding closed — scope will produce the tests.

findings.summary: isWorktreeDirectory false-positive risk in git submodules
findings.closed_reason: Handled: isWorktreeDirectory at worktree.ts:93-97 explicitly distinguishes worktrees from submodules by checking for '/worktrees/' in the .git file content. Comment at lines 93-95 documents the distinction. The only false positive requires a submodule literally named 'worktrees' — adversarial naming in a project also using git worktrees. Consequence would be confusing output, not data corruption.

findings.summary: detectWorktreeSlug path-based detection fragile if project root contains .ana/worktrees/
findings.closed_reason: Mitigated: primary detection at worktree.ts:114-121 uses worktree-meta.json (written at creation time) — reliable and not path-dependent. The path-based fallback at lines 123-130 is for legacy worktrees without meta.json only. The edge case (project root inside another project's worktree) requires nested Anatomia setups AND missing meta.json — both conditions simultaneously. Consequence would be wrong slug name in output, not data corruption.

findings.summary: proof.ts WRONG_BRANCH primary error still says 'Switch to main' even in worktree context
findings.closed_reason: Correct guidance is shown: WRONG_BRANCH error at proof.ts:800/1050/1342/1681 says 'Switch to main' (generic). The formatHint callback at lines 778/1028/1298/1637 adds 'You're in a worktree. Run from the main project directory' when isWorktreeDirectory() is true. The user sees BOTH — the hint provides the correct worktree-specific instruction. Making the primary message worktree-aware would require isWorktreeDirectory checks in createExitError — UX polish for an edge case that already has correct guidance in the hint.

entries.scope_summary: Every test assumes the artifact branch is `main`. The `artifactBranch` field in `ana.json` supports any branch name, and the runtime code reads it correctly via `readArtifactBranch()` — but zero tests exercise a non-main value. A team using `develop` as their artifact branch hits untested code paths in `work start`, `artifact save`, `work complete`, `work status`, and `proof` commands. Add parameterized tests proving non-main values work, and fix a display bug where init shows the git default branch instead of the configured artifact branch.

Covered by Rule 2 (enforcement tests for structural invariants).

entries.scope_summary: When a pipeline run has rejection cycles (FAIL → fix → PASS), intermediate artifacts are lost. The Verify agent correctly deletes previous reports before writing fresh ones — this ensures write independence across rounds. But the FAIL-round verify report, its companion YAML, and the pre-fix build report are gone by the time `artifact save` runs. The proof chain entry records `rejection_cycles` count and `previous_failures` summaries, but the full artifacts are permanently lost.

findings.summary: A014 test does not exercise actual archive failure (catch branch). Tests first-save no-op, not error recovery.
archivePreviousVersion uses string equality for content comparison — could produce false archives on Windows with CRLF line endings from git
findings.closed_reason: Direct fix prompt delivered to Think — normalize CRLF line endings before archive comparison at artifact.ts:353. Fix: diskContent.replace(/\r\n/g, '\n') === committedContent. No-op on macOS/Linux, prevents unnecessary archives on Windows with core.autocrlf=true. Awaiting Think execution.

findings.summary: No upper bound on round numbers — a slug that goes through many rejection cycles will accumulate archive files with no cleanup mechanism
findings.closed_reason: No practical impact: across 96 pipeline runs, the maximum archive round is _r2 (10 instances) and _r1 (40 instances). Archive files are created on re-save (not just rejection cycles — max rejection cycles is 1). The accumulation concern requires many rounds which don't occur. 50 total archive files across 96 runs is modest. No cleanup mechanism needed at this scale.

entries.scope_summary: V1 proved worktree isolation works — the trust model is enforced physically. But Build enters the worktree blind: no knowledge of which files have caused trouble before, and the proof chain is just a record nobody reads at build time. Meanwhile, phase timing lies — it measures gaps between artifact saves (which include developer idle time, sometimes days) instead of actual phase durations. And health display is missing the plan phase entirely.

entries.scope_summary: Fix 13 visual regressions between the locked handoff HTML and the shipped website. Every item was validated against both the handoff source (`Anatomia Landing Refresh handoff.html`) and the live implementation. The site is live at anatomia.dev — these differences are visible to anyone comparing the design reference.

findings.summary: tCompat hover state leaks through inversion — base .tile:hover sets border-color: var(--border)
findings.closed_reason: Not broken: .tCompat:hover at bento.module.css:122 explicitly sets border-color: transparent, overriding .tile:hover at line 61. Same specificity (0,1,0 + pseudo), .tCompat:hover wins by cascade order. Both rules in same CSS module file — no bundler reordering risk.

findings.summary: TetrisSnake trail fade math off by 2 ticks — fades to 0 at age 18 but filter removes at age 20
findings.closed_reason: Harmless: trail items at age 18-19 render with globalAlpha=0 (Math.max(0, 1-age/18) * 0.9 = 0) — canvas no-ops producing zero pixels. Items are removed at age 20. The 2-tick gap means ~2 invisible fillRect calls per frame on a decorative animation. Zero visual impact, negligible performance cost.

findings.summary: DPR cap at 2 added without spec guidance — prevents large canvas on 3x displays
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: DPR cap at 2 (TetrisSnake.tsx:50) is standard canvas performance practice. 3x DPR means 9x pixel count — capping at 2 prevents performance issues on high-DPR devices. Visual difference between 2x and 3x rendering on a decorative animation is imperceptible.

findings.summary: cardPriceUnit override added for highlighted card — not in spec but necessary for visibility
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: .cardHighlighted .cardPriceUnit override at pricing.module.css:75-77 is required for legibility. The highlighted card inverts to dark background (var(--fg-strong)) — without this override, the price unit text would be invisible. The color-mix ensures it matches the cardSub/cardFeature treatment.

findings.summary: TetrisSnake getComputedStyle called every frame — potential performance concern on low-end mobile
findings.closed_reason: Intentional per-frame read: getComputedStyle at TetrisSnake.tsx:44 reads --color-brand every frame (14fps) to track theme changes. Comment at line 42 documents the intent. getComputedStyle on document.documentElement with no pending style changes is O(1) — a lookup, not a recalculation. At 14 calls/sec, it adds ~0.14ms/sec total. The canvas drawing (600+ rects/frame) is the actual bottleneck on low-end devices, not one getComputedStyle call.

findings.summary: TetrisSnake placed blocks array grows unbounded between lap clears — no cap on array size
findings.closed_reason: Self-limiting: placed array at TetrisSnake.tsx:77 is bounded by stochastic clearing at line 85 (40% removed every 2 laps). For a 1200×600 canvas (perimeter ~360 cells), ~120 blocks added per lap, 240 per 2-lap cycle. Equilibrium at ~600 blocks (where 0.4 × N = 240 additions). 600 entries × ~24 bytes = ~14KB — negligible for a canvas animation. The array can't grow unbounded because removals always catch up to additions.

findings.summary: PostHog useEffect has no cleanup return — posthog.init() could be called on remount

findings.summary: Race condition in writeTimestamp: read-modify-write on .saves.json is not atomic

entries.scope_summary: Clean the edges of a fundamentally sound 2000+ test suite. Over 64 pipeline runs, tests have accumulated that don't test behavior: source-reading tests that grep `.ts` files for strings, archaeological tests verifying deleted code stays deleted, mislabeled tests whose names contradict their assertions, weak assertions using `toBeDefined()` on deterministic fields, and dead test helper exports. entries.scope_summary: Build and Verify agents run in worktrees but Claude Code's Write tool resolves absolute paths against the main tree. The agent writes `build_report.md` to the main tree. `artifact save` detects this but errors with manual recovery instructions instead of fixing it, leaving a stale copy on main. When `work complete` pulls the merged PR, git refuses because the untracked file would be overwritten. In re-build scenarios (verify FAIL, fix, new artifacts), the stale copy diverges from the PR content, and the existing content-match auto-clean (`be3374f`) can't help.

findings.summary: A005 EXDEV test doesn't exercise moveFileCrossFs — tests Node.js copyFileSync/unlinkSync directly instead of mocking renameSync to throw EXDEV
findings.closed_reason: Structural ESM limitation: moveFileCrossFs at artifact.ts:1032 is 10 lines — renameSync, catch EXDEV, copyFileSync+unlinkSync. ESM built-in modules have non-configurable exports, preventing mocking renameSync to throw EXDEV. The test at artifact.test.ts:3597 verifies the fallback operations (copy+delete) work correctly. The EXDEV error code check is POSIX standard, consistently implemented by Node.js. The function is correct by inspection — the untestable part is a 1-line error code comparison.

findings.summary: A008 sweep-failure test is a no-op — tests absence of sweep (no main tree copy), not an actual cleanup failure
findings.closed_reason: Test is valid despite misleading name: the test at artifact.test.ts:3681 proves saveArtifact succeeds from a worktree when there's nothing to sweep. The sweep's error handling (try/catch at lines 1508-1513) is a standard best-effort pattern — 'catch {}' swallows errors. Testing that catch swallows errors is testing the language, not the application. The cleanup failure path is correct by inspection: 1 line of catch {}.

findings.summary: moveFileCrossFs copy-then-delete is not atomic — if copyFileSync succeeds but unlinkSync fails, source file persists as a stale duplicate
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: moveFileCrossFs at artifact.ts:1031 tries renameSync (atomic) first, falls back to copyFileSync+unlinkSync only on EXDEV. This is the standard Node.js cross-filesystem move pattern. Non-atomic path requires cross-fs + permission error on delete — theoretical only.

findings.summary: Layer 3 planning artifact content-match reads file without try-catch — if file is deleted between filter and readFileSync, unhandled ENOENT crashes completeWork
findings.closed_reason: Fixed: wrapped readFileSync at work.ts:1305 in try-catch (read failure → allMatch=false, triggers safe 'inspect manually' path). Wrapped unlinkSync at work.ts:1320 in try-catch (best-effort, matching existing pattern at line 1287). Commit 5d665444.

findings.summary: Layer 2 post-save sweep calls getMainTreeRoot a second time — already computed in Layer 1 block but not threaded through
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: getMainTreeRoot called at artifact.ts:1201 (Layer 1 auto-move) and :1493 (Layer 2 post-save sweep) — 290 lines apart in different conditional phases. Single git rev-parse call, microseconds. Threading a variable through unrelated code blocks adds coupling for no gain.

findings.summary: Dogfood templates (.claude/agents/) also received pwd hint — not in contract file_changes but correct behavior
findings.closed_reason: upstream

build_concerns.summary: A005 EXDEV fallback not directly exercised in integration test — ESM module exports non-configurable
Layer 2 sweep runs unconditionally on every build-verify save — negligible overhead but always-check design

entries.scope_summary: Pipeline artifact saves (scope, plan, spec, contract, build-report, verify-report) and work completions push to main but only touch `.ana/` files. Every push triggers the full GitHub Actions test matrix — 6 test runners + 1 website check — against completely unchanged source code. 36% of the last 50 CI runs (18 of 50) were triggered by artifact-only commits. The pre-commit hook already skips these correctly; GitHub Actions does not.

entries.scope_summary: The website's mobile experience is broken in several visible ways: the ship log header text overlaps and garbles, the hamburger nav has no opaque background so content bleeds through, the hero meta row wraps awkwardly, ship log expanded rows lose all proof signal on narrow screens, the compatibility marquee has placeholder icons and a stale tool list, and the "Full proof chain" link is a 404. These are all on the primary marketing surface. Fix them, and do it responsively across phone, tablet, and desktop — not just "desktop and mobile."

findings.summary: Copilot/Cline use currentColor — renders as var(--fg) inside .glyph, works in both themes but contrast depends on --ink-15 background chip
findings.closed_reason: Intentional design: icons.tsx header (line 7) documents 'Copilot, Cline use currentColor for dark-mode safety (their brand colors are near-black).' The --ink-15 chip is deliberately subtle — 15% opacity dark (light mode) or 16% opacity white (dark mode). currentColor has good contrast against background, and the chip is transparent enough not to affect it. Considered design decision documented in the file header.

entries.scope_summary: Agents write scope fields inconsistently and nothing catches it. Kind was added to the scope template and wired through the CLI — but 1 of 4 post-fix scopes omitted it entirely, 1 wrote an invalid compound value (`fix + chore`), and all 4 produced MISSING in the proof chain because the CLI binary was never rebuilt after the fix merged. The same pattern applies to Size (10 of 70 scopes used non-standard values like `small-medium` or `medium (with explanation)`) and Multi-phase (4 of 70 had trailing context that would break strict parsing). The validator at `ana artifact save scope` checks ACs, Structural Analog, and Intent — but doesn't check any Complexity Assessment fields. Agents populate what they want, the pipeline accepts it silently, and data is lost downstream.

findings.summary: A016 only tests 'Feature' case variant, not 'FIX' — contract says both should be accepted
Console.error capture pattern repeated verbatim in 8 rejection tests — extraction into a helper would reduce duplication
findings.closed_reason: Test hygiene preference, not a problem: 12 console.error capture instances in artifact.test.ts use the standard vitest pattern (save→mock→capture→restore→assert). All 12 tests work correctly. The pattern hasn't changed in the project's lifetime. Extracting a helper would reduce maintenance if the pattern changes — but it's the standard approach and is unlikely to change. The duplication is localized (one test file) and each instance is self-contained.

findings.summary: Pre-existing scope validation tests (lines 697-746) still use plain toThrow() without checking error message content
Post-merge hook uses set -e but wraps build in if-guard — correct now, but fragile if future edits add unguarded commands
findings.closed_reason: Correct as-is: 17-line post-merge hook at .husky/post-merge has set -e with all commands either guarded (grep || true at line 9, if ! build at line 14) or infallible (echo, cd, variable assignment). The script is complete and does one thing — rebuild CLI on source changes. Future edits are unlikely, and the if-guard pattern is standard for set -e scripts. Comment at line 3 documents non-blocking intent.

entries.scope_summary: `ana work start` writes timestamps to `.saves.json` on the artifact branch without committing, creating dirty tracked state that blocks `git pull --rebase` for concurrent pipeline work. The user wants this fixed — it caused a confirmed failure during concurrent work on 2026-05-07 and is a recurring friction source. Secondary: comments and messages in the affected files say "main" when they mean "artifact branch," which is misleading for customers using a different artifact branch name.

findings.summary: A010 test creates untracked file after commit — doesn't test scoped staging during commit
commitSaves silently swallows commit failures — index.lock or other git errors invisible to user
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: silent catch at work.ts:2236 is by design — comment says 'don't block the user's workflow for a convenience commit.' commitSaves is best-effort.

findings.summary: A011 no-push test relies on absence of remote as indirect proof — no spy or mock verifying git push not called
findings.closed_reason: Intentional — no-push test at work.test.ts:3134 creates project without remote. If completeWork attempted push, it would throw (no remote configured). Test passing is sufficient proof no push was attempted.

findings.summary: commitSaves mixes runGit (throws) and spawnSync (returns status) for git operations — works correctly but inconsistent API usage
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: commitSaves uses runGit (throws, caught at line 2220) for staging and spawnSync (status check) for diff and commit. Both patterns are correct for their use — runGit early-returns on nothing-to-stage, spawnSync checks exit codes. Not worth normalizing.

findings.summary: Race condition in writeTimestamp still present — read-modify-write on .saves.json is not atomic (pre-existing, from Fix Pipeline Phase Timing)
findings.closed_reason: upstream

entries.scope_summary: The pipeline manages every step from scope to PR creation, but merge is unmanaged. When developers delegate the merge to an agent (which they inevitably do), the agent improvises with raw `gh` commands — using `--delete-branch` (worktree conflict), `--squash` (opinionated), `--admin` (bypasses branch protection) — producing confusing errors and inconsistent state. Observed live: the verify agent hit every one of these failure modes in sequence when asked to merge PR #95. The user wants a structured merge path that agents can use without improvising.

findings.summary: A020, A021 assert on source code content instead of testing behavior
findings.closed_reason: Accepted: A020 at work.test.ts:3222 reads work.ts source and asserts retry message strings exist. The source-code assertion is weak (matches anywhere in file) but the behavioral alternative requires mocking spawnSync mid-function to fail only at the git commit step — non-trivial because earlier git operations (pull, add) would also fail with the same mock. The test pragmatically verifies the message exists; the commit failure path is structurally difficult to test.

findings.summary: No tests verify --json output for any of the 7 merge failure paths
JSON.parse on gh pr view stdout has no try/catch — malformed response crashes
findings.closed_reason: Stale: try-catch added at work.ts:1138-1145 after the original work-complete-merge commit (ae45b692). JSON.parse failure now caught with error message and --json envelope support.

findings.summary: getNextAction multi-line return breaks status output formatting — second line lacks indentation and styling
findings.closed_reason: Scoped in fix-work-saves-compat: spec Fix 3 changes getNextAction return type to string | string[]. Ready-to-merge returns 2-element array instead of newline-joined string. Caller at line 659 updated to format each element with arrow prefix and indentation. Contract A008-A009.

findings.summary: Auto-merge enabled path writes plain text to stdout before JSON output — pollutes stdout for --json consumers
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-merge-json-pollution scope (merged PR #131). All merge-path console.log calls now guarded with if (!options?.json) at work.ts:1307, 1321, 1390. No plain text reaches stdout in --json mode. Stale across 20 subsequent entries.

findings.closed_reason: Intentional: work-merge.test.ts was separated from work.test.ts because vi.mock(node:child_process) at module level would affect all tests in the file (explained in header lines 4-6). Creating a separate test file for module-level mock isolation is standard Vitest practice. Reasonable deviation from contract file_changes.

entries.scope_summary: Replace the existing Bento section with a new "System" section that shows what `ana init` ships into a repo: agents, skills, context files, and the CLI. The design is a four-drawer accordion with file trees and a man-page CLI mock. The handoff HTML is approved and responsive-tested at 375/768/1280. This scope covers re-engineering it into modular Next.js components following existing website patterns, fixing factual errors in the handoff, and making key data (version, command count) maintainable.

findings.summary: Drawer sectionRef prop is dead code — defined but never passed by SystemSection
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in 6000e283 — removed dead sectionRef prop from Drawer.tsx. Prop was never passed by SystemSection (the only consumer at line 54). IntersectionObserver at line 40 now uses containerRef.current directly instead of sectionRef?.current ?? containerRef.current. Zero behavioral change.

findings.summary: SectionThread breathe prop applies undefined animate-breathe CSS class — no-op if invoked
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in 6000e283 — removed broken breathe prop from SectionThread.tsx. Prop applied nonexistent 'animate-breathe' CSS class (the CSS module class is styles.breathe in system.module.css, used directly by SystemSection at line 58). Prop was never passed as true by any consumer (ScanSlab.tsx:160 omits it). The arrow span now renders without a className.

findings.summary: ManPage footer date '2026-05' is a hardcoded string — will go stale monthly
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: ManPage date '2026-05' at ManPage.tsx:60 is a decorative man-page mock footer. In real man pages, the date represents the last revision date, not the current date. Automating it (build-time Date) would make it less authentic — real man pages are manually dated when content is revised. Update when CLI commands change, like a real man page maintainer would.

findings.summary: SystemSection closer does not use SectionThread component — pattern duplication
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: SystemSection closer (lines 57-62) and SectionThread serve different visual purposes. SectionThread is a horizontal section-to-section connector (→ arrow, Tailwind classes, mono text). The closer is a vertical call-to-action within a section (↓ arrow, CSS module classes, styled link). Different layout, different styling system, different semantic purpose. Not duplication.

findings.summary: copy.ts systemThread key is defined but never consumed by any component — dead data
findings.closed_reason: Fixed: removed dead systemThread object from copy.ts. Zero references in any TSX file — grep for systemThread returns only the definition. Type (Copy = typeof copy) auto-shrinks. Commit 3b023833.

findings.summary: color-mix() CSS function used for hover effects — ~93% browser support, older browsers get no hover feedback
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: color-mix() at system.module.css:198 is one of many usages site-wide — globals.css:67-68 uses it for core design tokens (--brand-soft, --brand-deep), bento.module.css uses it, and Tailwind 4 itself depends on it. This is a site-wide CSS feature commitment, not an isolated risk. Browsers without color-mix() would have broader visual issues than this one toggle hover.

entries.feature: CLI commits scoped to intended paths

assertions.says: The work complete commit only contains the files it intended to stage
Pre-existing staged files remain staged after a scoped commit
The work complete commit includes the archived plan directory
The work complete commit includes the proof chain
The single artifact save commit uses scoped pathspecs
The multi artifact save commit uses scoped pathspecs
The single artifact diff check uses scoped pathspecs
The multi artifact diff check uses scoped pathspecs
The saves-only commit uses a scoped pathspec
The saves-only diff check uses a scoped pathspec
The proof commit uses scoped pathspecs from options.files
The recovery commit uses scoped pathspecs
Orphan file removals are included in the scoped multi-artifact commit
A clean index produces identical behavior with scoped commits

entries.scope_summary: Every `git commit` in the CLI commits the entire staging index, not the specific files it staged. Pre-existing staged changes from other sessions, manual operations, or concurrent pipeline work leak into unrelated commits. Observed: `work complete commit-work-start-timestamps` (commit `bb2ee1f`) swept `polish-scan-copy`'s staged deletions into its commit — files from a different work item deleted by a different terminal session. The user wants every CLI commit to contain exactly what was intended.

findings.summary: No integration tests for artifact.ts or proof.ts scoped commit sites — 9 of 14 assertions verified by source inspection only
findings.closed_reason: Intentional — 9 of 14 assertions verified by source inspection because the scope checks git commit call-site arguments. Source inspection is the appropriate method for verifying path-scoping in commit calls. Integration tests for each commit site would require full git repo setup per call site — high cost, low marginal confidence.

findings.summary: git commit -- uses --only semantics (working tree, not index) — safe because git add and commit are adjacent synchronous calls, but undocumented assumption
findings.closed_reason: Standard git usage: 'git commit -- {paths}' at artifact.ts:1465 commits only the listed paths from the index. The -- separator is standard git pathspec syntax, not --only semantics (finding's characterization is imprecise). git add (lines 1394-1446) and git commit are synchronous with no interleaving — single-threaded CLI guarantees the index isn't modified between them. Scoped commits intentionally don't touch user's staged work.

findings.summary: Site 2 stages plan.md with absolute path via runGit but tracks relative path in stagedPaths — works correctly but mixed path convention
findings.closed_reason: Works correctly: git add at artifact.ts:1807 receives absolute path, stagedPaths at line 1808 tracks relative path. Both refer to the same file — git normalizes absolute paths to repo-relative internally. spawnSync passes args as an array (no shell), so spaces in paths are safe. The mixed convention is cosmetic — git handles both forms identically.

findings.summary: Test uses toContain('completed/') for path matching — works in controlled test but would false-positive if any other path contained 'completed/'
findings.closed_reason: Intentional — toContain('completed/') at work.test.ts:1243 runs against git diff-tree output in a single-slug fixture. No other path can contain 'completed/' in this test. The primary assertion is not.toContain('unrelated.txt') — the completed/ check is a sanity confirmation.

findings.summary: commitSaves silently swallows commit failures — pre-existing, still present (see proof context)
findings.closed_reason: upstream

build_concerns.summary: Contract assertions A005-A013 verified by code inspection only, no dedicated per-site tests
git commit -- <paths> uses --only semantics (working tree, not index) — safe with synchronous staging but a latent assumption

entries.feature: Init must surface scan quality and pipeline readiness

assertions.says: A degraded scan shows a warning instead of a success checkmark
A degraded scan tells the user what analysis was lost in plain language
A clean scan confirms no gaps were detected
Tree-sitter failures are described in human terms, not technical jargon
The raw tree-sitter error message is not shown to the user
Each blind spot shows its area and what went wrong
Each blind spot shows how to resolve it
No blind spots means no blind spot section is shown
Missing git user name produces a warning with a fix command
Missing git user email produces a warning with a fix command
Git user checks are skipped when git is not installed
Missing GitHub CLI produces a warning that says the pipeline still works
The remote warning now explains what it means for the pipeline
Preflight warnings are captured in the result, not just printed
No new check prevents init from completing
Pipeline warnings appear in the success message before next steps
No warnings means no pipeline readiness section
The setup agent checks environment tools after completing setup
The setup agent checks git identity configuration
The setup agent is told not to install software without permission
Total scan failure still shows the existing graceful message
Scan engine blind spot messages are not modified

entries.scope_summary: `ana init` tells users what it found but never what it missed. Tree-sitter failures are silently swallowed — the user sees "Analysis complete" with a checkmark and walks away thinking they got a full deep scan when they got surface-tier only. Init also never checks whether the tools needed for the pipeline (`gh`, `git user.name`/`user.email`) are available, leaving users to discover gaps at the worst time — mid-pipeline, after investing real work. The user wants init to be honest about scan quality and upfront about what the pipeline needs.

findings.summary: A014 uses toBeGreaterThan(0) — weak assertion when specific count is knowable
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A014 test at init-preflight.test.ts:156 is named 'returns warnings array in PreflightResult' — its purpose is proving the array exists and is populated, not verifying exact count. toBeGreaterThan(0) achieves that purpose and is resilient to new preflight checks being added. The A015 test provides a stricter bound (toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(3)) for the all-fail scenario.

findings.summary: A015 uses toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(3) — specific count should be exactly 3 (user.name, user.email, gh)
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A015 at init-preflight.test.ts:180 uses toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(3) — deliberately resilient to new preflight checks being added. The fixture triggers exactly 3 warnings (user.name, user.email, gh — comment confirms). The assertion catches warning suppression (count drops below 3) while tolerating new additions. entries.scope_summary: The configurability story is incomplete. Users can't safely store custom config (re-init silently strips unknown keys), the verify agent's skill loading is inconsistent with every other pipeline agent, and there's no CLI command for reading or writing ana.json. These three changes give Anatomia a real configurability surface — user data is preserved, the agent system is internally consistent, and configuration has a proper CLI interface. This also unblocks the docs site configurability guide, which currently has to describe "edit JSON by hand."

The type widening is the tradeoff for preservation. Comments at lines 25-28 document the cross-check with createAnaJson. Object.keys enumeration in config displayAll (line 192) SHOULD include unknown fields — that's the desired behavior for 'show all config.' Consumers access known fields by name, not dynamically.

entries.scope_summary: After a FAIL verification on a multi-phase build, the fix cycle gets permanently stuck at `needs-fixes` even when the fix build completes successfully. AnaVerify reports "nothing to verify." The pipeline is dead until a human manually intervenes. This happened on `configurability-improvements` — the first real multi-phase build to go through a FAIL-fix-re-verify cycle. It will happen to every customer who hits a verify rejection on any multi-phase work item, and the single-spec path has the same underlying fragility.

findings.closed_reason: Scoped in fix-work-saves-compat: spec Fix 1 adds phaseNum === 1 guard to saves.json fallback at both completeWork (line 1553) and getWorkStatus (line 472). findings.summary: Stage detection tests use hardcoded timestamps with 1-hour gaps — no boundary test for equal timestamps
Auto-rename overwrites numbered file unconditionally — if the unnumbered file is stale or corrupt, the good numbered version is destroyed
findings.closed_reason: Current ordering is correct — closing, not fixing. The finding suggested swapping archive-before-rename, but this would BREAK the mechanism: (1) archive compares disk with HEAD, (2) before rename, disk matches HEAD (both are original), (3) archive skips (no change), (4) rename overwrites original with fix content, (5) original lost with no archive. The CURRENT order (rename first → archive second) works: after rename, disk has fix content, archive compares fix vs HEAD (original), they differ, original is archived as _r1. The 'data loss' scenario requires an uncommitted artifact from a crashed Build — outside normal pipeline flow.

entries.scope_summary: The kind system classifies work as `feature`, `fix`, or `chore`. These three values are accurate but incomplete — there's no way to distinguish routine feature work from significant new capabilities. A filter button added to an existing page and an entirely new authentication system are both "feature." This matters because kind flows into the proof chain, the website ship log, and will soon drive branch prefix selection (Scope 2: kind-aware branch prefixes). A fourth kind, `milestone`, gives Ana and developers a way to signal "this is announcement-worthy" — enabling downstream consumers to surface significant work differently from routine work.

entries.scope_summary: Branch prefixes are static — one string for all work items. But the system already knows the work type: Ana classifies every scope as `feature`, `fix`, `chore`, or `milestone` (after Scope 1 ships). Teams with git conventions like `fix/`, `feature/`, `chore/` can't express those conventions through Anatomia. The branch always gets whatever single prefix is configured, regardless of what the work actually is. This scope makes `branchPrefix` kind-aware: a map from kind to prefix, so `fix/auth-timeout` and `feature/add-export` happen automatically based on what Ana already knows.

findings.closed_reason: Finding's premise is incorrect: rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD returns 'HEAD' (exitCode 0) on detached state, not '(unknown)'. branchName is 'HEAD' which is a valid git ref — rev-list, log, and all downstream commands work correctly with it. The '(unknown)' fallback at worktree.ts:310 only triggers on rev-parse failure (e.g., corrupt git state), not on detached HEAD.

'(unknown)' at line 1926. getCurrentBranch is already imported at line 22 and used at 4 other call sites. Verified: no more inline branch-reading.
