entries.scope_summary: Worktrees don't know when they're behind main. When multiple pipelines run concurrently — or when main advances for any reason while a worktree is active — the build and verification happen against stale code. The developer discovers this only at merge time ("branch is behind"), after wasting an entire build+verify cycle. Worse: if the PR merges cleanly despite being behind (no conflicting files), the proof chain records a PASS that was verified against different code than what shipped. The proof is weak and nobody knows.

findings.summary: commitsBehind uses origin/artifactBranch but commitCount uses bare artifactBranch — asymmetric ref comparison
findings.closed_reason: Intentional asymmetry: commitCount at worktree.ts:318 uses local artifactBranch because it answers 'commits since branching' (branch point is local). commitsBehind at line 332 uses origin/artifactBranch because it answers 'how far behind remote' (what needs rebasing). Using local for behind-count would miss remote-only commits. Using remote for commit-count would under-count after local main commits. Each question requires its correct ref.

findings.summary: printExistingWorktree duplicates commitsBehind rev-list logic from getWorktreeInfo — now two inline computations duplicated instead of one
findings.closed_reason: Scoped in fix-work-saves-compat: spec Fix 2 replaces printExistingWorktree's commitsBehind rev-list computation with getWorktreeInfo, which already computes commitsBehind (WorktreeInfo includes the field — verified type at worktree.ts:38-45). Contract A006-A007.

findings.summary: A008 JSON test asserts typeof === 'number' not a specific value — passes even if commitsBehind computation is broken
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in 752da396 — replaced typeof === 'number' with toBe(0) at work.test.ts:663. Test fixture creates worktree with no commits ahead, so commitsBehind is deterministically 0.

findings.summary: A005 git failure test relies on absent origin/main ref rather than injecting a failure — indirect coverage of the catch path
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A005 test at worktree.test.ts:492 creates a worktree without origin/main — git rev-list fails, catch block triggers, commitsBehind defaults to 0. Line 502 asserts toBe(0). The test uses real git commands in a fresh temp repo — absence of origin/main IS the failure condition. Comment at line 498-499 explains: 'In test env without origin/main, it should still default to 0.'

findings.closed_reason: upstream

findings.summary: pr.ts fetch adds network latency to every PR creation — no timeout or skip mechanism
findings.closed_reason: Acceptable tradeoff: git fetch at pr.ts:179 ensures the behind-warning is accurate (shows commits on remote that need rebasing). On offline networks, git fails after DNS timeout (~5-10s) and the catch block continues silently. On hanging networks, spawnSync blocks until git times out or user Ctrl+C — but this affects all git network operations, not just ana. The fetch is best-effort and the alternative (stale behind-count from unfetched refs) produces misleading warnings.

entries.scope_summary: `git branch -a` marks branches checked out in linked worktrees with a `+ ` prefix. `getWorkBranch()` at `work.ts:144` strips the `* ` prefix (current branch) but not the `+ ` prefix (worktree branch). The result: when a worktree is active, `getWorkBranch` returns `"+ feature/slug"` — an invalid git ref — to every caller. Build reports become invisible, pipeline stage detection breaks, `--merge` fails, and `work status` shows wrong stages for every work item with an active worktree.

findings.summary: No dedicated @ana tag for A003 — existing test satisfies the assertion but is tagged @ana A010 from a prior contract
findings.closed_reason: A003 has dedicated @ana A003 tags at 5 other test locations (lines 326, 972, 2161, 3288, 4587). Coverage is complete. This test at line 781 also exercises A003 behavior but is tagged A010 — cosmetic tagging gap, not a coverage gap.

findings.summary: A004 and A005 are source-inspection-only assertions — no behavioral test verifies the regex actually strips + markers in git.ts (only work.ts path is integration-tested)
findings.closed_reason: Covered through integration: detectBranches at git.ts:100 is called by detectGitInfo which is tested by git-activity.test.ts with real git repos. Real repos have '* main' output — the * stripping at line 109 IS exercised. The + marker (worktree branches) uses the same regex character class [*+] — if * stripping works, + stripping works. The function is 18 lines with standard regex. Direct unit test would add a mock for a pattern already exercised by integration.

findings.summary: No mutual exclusion between featureBranch and worktree options in createWorkTestProject — setting both creates branch via checkout then fails on worktree add
findings.closed_reason: No test passes both featureBranch and worktree — only one test uses worktree:true (line 739) and it doesn't set featureBranch. The conflict is theoretical. If triggered, git worktree add would fail immediately with 'branch already exists' — obvious, not silent. Test helper API design concern, not a bug.

findings.summary: Integration test covers work.ts parsing path end-to-end but git.ts detectBranches is not exercised by any new test — the fix is verified only by source inspection
findings.closed_reason: Same as fix-worktree-branch-parsing-C2 — companion finding about the same code. detectBranches is exercised through detectGitInfo integration tests. The git.ts fix (adding + to the regex character class) is verified by the same reasoning: [*+] is a single character class where testing * exercises the class. The integration test path through scanProject → detectGitInfo → detectBranches covers the function end-to-end.

The user wants to clean house — eliminate the noise permanently with minimal regression risk.

The scenario requires manually editing committed planning artifacts on the artifact branch, which is outside normal pipeline workflow.

findings.summary: process.cwd() in loader DATA_PATH assumes Next.js runs from website/ root — correct for Next.js build, fragile if loaders are ever called from tests or scripts
findings.closed_reason: Standard Next.js pattern: process.cwd() at proofs.ts:6 returns website/ root in all Next.js contexts (server components, build, dev server). The loaders are website-only — never imported by CLI or scripts. The data/ directory is a build artifact consumed only by Next.js. The fragility concern requires a standalone script importing docs-data loaders from the wrong cwd — no such script exists and the module isn't designed for standalone use.

findings.summary: Callout label stores titlecase (Rule/Note), relies on CSS text-transform for uppercase display
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: Callout stores titlecase ('Rule'/'Note') at Callout.tsx:11-12 with CSS textTransform:uppercase at line 61 for display. Standard web pattern — content stores natural case, CSS transforms for presentation. Benefits: screen readers read 'Rule' not 'RULE', design changes require CSS-only edits, graceful degradation if CSS fails.

findings.summary: Catch-all route renamed from [[...slug]] to [...slug] — not specified in spec but necessary
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: catch-all route changed from [[...slug]] (optional) to [...slug] (required) because /docs has a dedicated page.tsx. Optional catch-all would conflict with the explicit page. Finding says 'necessary.' This is Next.js routing infrastructure — the builder made the correct routing decision.

findings.summary: ResourceStrip uses <a> for Manifesto link (internal anatomia.dev URL) instead of Next.js Link
findings.closed_reason: Intentional design: ResourceStrip presents all 3 resources as outbound links (external: true, ↗ arrows in names). The Manifesto at anatomia.dev/manifesto IS a Next.js route, but the component treats it as an external resource — consistent with the other two (GitHub, npm). Using <a> for same-domain URLs is acceptable when the UX intent is 'outbound resource' not 'internal navigation.' The layout boundary crossing (docs → marketing) means Link produces a similar navigation experience.

entries.scope_summary: Pipeline timing records inaccurate build/verify splits for any proof with multi-phase builds or rejection cycles. A 3-phase, 108-minute pipeline run (content-pages) initially recorded 0 minutes due to a key-lookup bug. The hotfix (`getLatestTime`) fixed the lookup but still produces wrong splits: for dynamic-pages (2 phases), it records build=56m/verify=41m when the accurate values are build=46m/verify=22m. The same conflation affects all 19 rejected proofs (21% of the chain). The total is always correct — it's the build/verify attribution that's wrong, with verify time counted as build time.

findings.summary: Non-null assertion on missing verify phase — verifyPhases[i-1]! crashes if verify-report-(N-1) missing when build-report-N exists
findings.closed_reason: Fixed: extracted verifyPhases[i-1] to prevVerify variable with null guard at proofSummary.ts:1643. Missing verify phase now produces null segStart, skipped by existing guard at line 1648 instead of crashing. Commit 60ead5bd.

findings.summary: writeSaveMetadata export scope widened for tests — only consumed by test files, widens module public API
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: writeSaveMetadata at artifact.ts:47 is exported for unit test access — used in-file at lines 1427, 1432, 1833, 1839 (same-file calls don't need export) and imported by artifact.test.ts and proofSummary.test.ts for 8+ unit tests. Same pattern as branchExists, SLUG_PATTERN, formatHumanReadable, isNewerVersion — exports for thorough unit testing. 6+ prior closures in this session establish the precedent.

findings.summary: Unbounded history array growth — each rejection cycle appends with no cap
findings.closed_reason: Bounded by pipeline workflow: history at artifact.ts:75 grows one entry per re-save. Typical scopes produce 0-2 history entries (max observed: 3 from content-pages investigation). The pipeline doesn't re-save the same artifact repeatedly — sequential phases save different artifact types. 3 entries × ~80 bytes = ~240 bytes. The 'unbounded' concern requires manual repetitive saves that don't occur in pipeline workflow. Connected to archive behavior investigated earlier in this session.

findings.summary: A019 asserts on source code content — reads proofSummary.ts and checks string patterns instead of behavioral assertion
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A019 at proofSummary.test.ts:4061-4078 is an enforcement test — ensures MAX_PHASE_MS is declared exactly once in computeTiming (consolidation not undone). Source content assertion is the only way to enforce structural invariants. Same pattern as execSync enforcement, template content tests, blind-spot enforcement — 5+ precedents closed this session.

But it can't ship: search is a disabled button, the AI surface is placeholder links pointing at `#`, 10 hardcoded numbers are already stale (78 → 90 proofs), and there's no build safety net for link rot. This scope makes the site production-ready — users can find content, the AI links work, the numbers stay current, and the build catches broken links.

findings.summary: Search index fetched on every overlay open without cache invalidation awareness — 69KB JSON loaded client-side

entries.scope_summary: The think phase in pipeline timing systematically undercounts. Every proof shows 1-2 minutes of "think" time because the clock starts at `ana work start` (when the slug directory is created), not when the Ana conversation actually began. The real thinking — investigation, navigation, tradeoff discussion, scope drafting — happens before `work start`. A 45-minute scoping conversation records as 1 minute.

findings.summary: Conditional PID guard makes 8 tests potential no-ops in environments where getClaudePid() returns null
A015 reads source code instead of testing runtime behavior — pragmatic for Commander registration
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A015 at work.test.ts:4834 is an enforcement test verifying --session flag is registered on the status subcommand (line 4841) and NOT on start or complete (lines 4845, 4848). Source-code matching against Commander registration patterns is more stable than parsing help output. Finding says 'pragmatic for Commander registration.' Same enforcement pattern closed 6+ times this session.

findings.summary: A008 ordering verified by inspection only — no test enforces delete-before-use sequence
findings.closed_reason: Ordering is immaterial: even if read→use→delete instead of read→delete→use (work.ts:1848-1850), the worst case is two simultaneous startWork calls reading the same session file. Both would get the same timestamp — which is correct since they're in the same Claude session. The session file records 'when did this Claude session start' — that value is the same for all slugs started in the same session. The test at line 4714 proves the file IS deleted, which is the important invariant.

findings.summary: Async/sync IO inconsistency — session file written with fsPromises.writeFile but read with fs.readFileSync
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: fsPromises.writeFile at work.ts:684 (async getWorkStatus) and fs.readFileSync at work.ts:1848 (sync delete-then-use in getNextAction). Different functions, different contexts — async write is correct for async function, sync read is correct for immediate-delete pattern.

findings.summary: spawnSync used instead of spec-recommended execSync — better choice, structured exit code handling
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: finding itself says spawnSync is 'better choice, structured exit code handling.' The implementation deviated from spec by using a better pattern. No issue to fix.

entries.scope_summary: The pipeline Gantt chart on proof detail pages always renders 4 bars (Think, Plan, Build, Verify) regardless of how many phases a proof went through. A 3-phase proof like content-pages (108 minutes across 3 build/verify cycles) shows a single "Build 57m" bar and "Verify 30m" bar — hiding that phase 1 took 32+7 minutes, phase 2 took 14+13 minutes, and phase 3 took 11+10 minutes. The temporal shape of the pipeline run is invisible.

findings.summary: Gantt bar assertions (A014-A018, A022) test a re-implemented copy of buildGanttBars, not the production function in PipelineGantt.tsx
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by Think direct commit — removed opacity assertions and re-implementation from Gantt bar tests in proofSummary.test.ts. The test re-implemented buildGanttBars from PipelineGantt.tsx (cross-package boundary), and the production function changed to progressive opacity (commit 99e2e862) causing drift. Opacity is a rendering concern — CLI tests now test timing computation only, not website rendering decisions.

findings.summary: buildGanttBars and GanttBar exported from PipelineGantt.tsx but never imported — YAGNI exports for potential cross-package testing that doesn't happen
findings.closed_reason: Dead exports: buildGanttBars and GanttBar at PipelineGantt.tsx:22/39 are exported but never imported — zero consumers outside the file. buildGanttBars is used internally (line 121), GanttBar is its return type. Website has no test suite. Anticipatory exports for nonexistent tests — harmless but unnecessary.

findings.summary: OPACITY_MAP duplicates opacity values already in STAGES array — two sources of truth for the same constants
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in 99e2e862 — replaced OPACITY_MAP and STAGES.opacity with progressiveOpacity(index, count) function at PipelineGantt.tsx:16-18. Opacity now interpolates 0.55→1.0 across all bars based on position, not stage type. Works for any bar count (4/6/8). Eliminates the two-source-of-truth duplication entirely.

findings.summary: formatHumanReadable exported solely for test access — was previously private, scope widened for testing
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: formatHumanReadable at proof.ts:234 is used internally (line 699 for proof show) and imported by 2 test files for direct unit testing of rendering output. Exporting rendering functions for test access is standard — same pattern as branchExists (closed in this session). Testing formatting directly is better than testing through CLI output capture.

findings.summary: Multi-phase timeline text derives phase count via Math.max on filtered segments — works correctly but couples rendering to segment internals when entry.phases field exists for this purpose
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: segment derivation at proof/[slug]/page.tsx:92 is MORE robust than entry.phases — entry.phases only exists on 3/96 entries while segment data exists for all multi-phase entries. The conditional at line 91 (some(s => s.phase != null)) guards against empty segment sets. Coupling to segments is coupling to the actual source of truth, not an internal detail.

findings.summary: 60px label column may be tight for 'VERIFY 3' at 10.5px mono with 0.06em letter-spacing — fits now but fragile for higher phase counts
findings.closed_reason: No current impact: 60px label column at PipelineGantt.tsx:135 fits all observed labels. Max phases in proof chain is 3 — 'VERIFY 3' at 10.5px mono is ~56px, fits with 4px margin. The overflow concern requires 10+ phases which has never occurred (max is 3). Even if overflow happened, the 14px grid gap provides buffer before visual overlap — text would eat into gap space, not the bar column.

findings.summary: Cumulative left-offset rounding (Math.round per-bar) can exceed 100% with 8+ bars — known pre-existing issue from proof context, now higher risk with multi-phase rendering

Updating 90→93 proofs and 19→21 rejections during a build is routine content refresh, not a spec deviation. These tags exist specifically to be updated.

entries.feature: Commit hygiene checks at build-report save

assertions.says: Hygiene checks run when saving a build report
Hygiene checks do not run for non-build-report saves
Batch save via saveAllArtifacts also triggers hygiene checks
No additional git diff calls are made during hygiene checks
A lockfile changed without its manifest is flagged
A lockfile changed alongside its manifest is not flagged
Any package.json in a monorepo satisfies the lockfile requirement
A committed file containing an API key is flagged
Secrets in test files are not flagged
Secret regex state resets between files so all matches are found
A file with merge conflict markers is flagged
A file without conflict markers is not flagged
A .env file in the branch diff is flagged
A .env.example file in the diff is not flagged
A .env.test file in the diff is not flagged

Proof summary includes commit hygiene findings from saves.json
The proof chain entry includes commit hygiene data
Proof card shows commit hygiene section when findings exist
Proof card omits commit hygiene section when no findings exist
Empty modules_touched produces no findings and no errors
Missing saves.json is handled gracefully without errors

entries.scope_summary: Build agents make normal `git commit` calls during the build phase — code changes, test additions, dependency updates. These commits go through whatever hooks the project has (or doesn't have). But `ana artifact save build-report` commits with `--no-verify`, bypassing all hooks. Between these two commit types, there's no mechanical check that inspects the full branch diff for pipeline-specific footguns.

findings.summary: A002 test is tautological — verifies key absence without calling the function, not the gating conditional
findings.closed_reason: Acceptable: the A002 test at commit-hygiene.test.ts:257 IS tautological — it asserts the test helper's output doesn't contain commit_hygiene, not that saveArtifact skips hygiene checks for non-build-report types. But the production gating is a 1-line conditional (artifact.ts:1437: baseType === 'build-report'). The POSITIVE path (build-report triggers checks) is thoroughly tested at lines 61-133. The tautological test is harmless noise — it doesn't reduce confidence in the production conditional.

findings.summary: A017 uses toHaveProperty (existence) instead of asserting specific values — passes on any object shape
findings.closed_reason: Correct assertion type: A017 at commit-hygiene.test.ts:236 tests SCHEMA compliance — does the finding object have check/file/severity/message keys. toHaveProperty is the right assertion for schema tests. VALUE correctness is separately tested by A004 (toBe('lockfile-desync')), A005, A006 etc. A017 documents the expected shape; value tests verify specific outputs. Together they cover structure + values. Asserting values in A017 would duplicate A004.

findings.summary: A019 is type-level only — verifies ProofChainEntry accepts commit_hygiene, not that writeProofChain actually reads and writes it
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A019 at commit-hygiene.test.ts:416 is a type-sentinel test — verifies ProofChainEntry type accepts commit_hygiene field. TypeScript compilation enforces the type contract. Comment at lines 412-415 explains 'verified structurally.' The behavioral path (writeProofChain reads commit_hygiene from saves) is covered through completeWork integration. Same pattern as PipelineStats type sentinel (closed this session).

findings.summary: A024 tests the same function call as A001 — doesn't exercise saveAllArtifacts code path, just calls runCommitHygieneChecks directly
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A024 at commit-hygiene.test.ts:308 calls runCommitHygieneChecks directly because the function is call-site independent — same behavior whether called from saveArtifact (line 1439) or saveAllArtifacts (line 1732). Comment at lines 309-310 explains: 'verifies the function works regardless of call site.' The wiring (both callers invoke it) is verified by source inspection.

findings.summary: CommitHygieneFinding and runCommitHygieneChecks exported for test access — widens module public API
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: CommitHygieneFinding and runCommitHygieneChecks exported at artifact.ts:185/230 for unit test access. runCommitHygieneChecks is called in production at lines 1439 and 1732 (same-file calls don't need export) but is a standalone testable unit with a dedicated test file (commit-hygiene.test.ts, 20+ tests). Same pattern as writeSaveMetadata, branchExists — 7+ precedents this session.

findings.summary: commit_hygiene type duplicated in three locations (proof.ts, proofSummary.ts, work.ts inline) rather than imported from a shared definition
Proof context finding 'writeSaveMetadata export scope widened for tests' now also applies to CommitHygieneFinding + runCommitHygieneChecks — same pattern growing
findings.closed_reason: upstream

findings.summary: Secret scan reads full file content for every non-test file in modules_touched — large binary files or generated bundles would be read entirely into memory
findings.closed_reason: Acceptable: secret scan at artifact.ts:266 reads modules_touched files (build-modified source files from git diff). Binary files are rare in modules_touched (gitignored build output isn't tracked). readFileSync('utf-8') on binaries produces garbled text that won't match secret patterns. Large text files (JSON fixtures) are handled efficiently — Node.js regex scans multi-MB strings in milliseconds.

build_concerns.summary: runCommitHygieneChecks and CommitHygieneFinding exported for test access — widens artifact.ts public API
GitHub push protection blocks sk_live_ test strings — tests use phc_ (PostHog) keys instead, which are less recognizable as 'secret-like' to reviewers
History squashed to single commit due to push protection on sk_live_ in earlier commits — original two-commit structure lost

entries.feature: Run build command during worktree creation

assertions.says: Worktree creation runs the project's build command automatically

All worktree setup steps complete even when the build fails
Projects without a build command skip the build step

The worktree result includes a build status field

Failed build status is recorded in the worktree context file
The build step runs after env files are linked

entries.scope_summary: Build agents enter fresh worktrees and hit 283 test failures (MODULE_NOT_FOUND) because `dist/` is gitignored and `createWorktree()` never runs the project's build command. The developer wants worktrees to be ready for baseline testing the moment they're created — deps installed, env linked, artifacts built.

findings.summary: No timeout on spawnSync — hanging build command blocks worktree creation indefinitely
findings.closed_reason: Fixed: added timeout: 300000 (5 min) to spawnSync at worktree.ts:463. On timeout, spawnSync returns status: null, signal: SIGTERM — existing status === 0 check correctly treats this as build failure. Worktree creation continues with buildSucceeded: false. Commit 3ba6e5df.

findings.summary: Empty string build command passes typeof guard and executes spawnSync('')
findings.closed_reason: Fixed: added empty/whitespace guard at worktree.ts:454 — typeof check now includes !buildCmd.trim(). Empty string previously passed typeof guard; on Node 25 spawnSync throws (caught by try-catch), on older Node runs no-op shell command returning exit 0. Guard makes behavior consistent across versions. Commit 3ba6e5df.

findings.summary: getBuildCommandString re-reads ana.json instead of receiving command from runBuildCommand — duplicate I/O with misleading 'pnpm run build' fallback
getBuildCommandString fallback 'pnpm run build' is unreachable in practice — dead code path
findings.closed_reason: Not dead code — defensive fallback: getBuildCommandString at worktree.ts:430-432 returns 'pnpm run build' when commands.build is missing or non-string. Worktrees can be created from branches with older ana.json versions that lack commands.build, or from corrupted configs. Both fallback paths (line 430: non-string value, line 432: missing/malformed file) handle real edge cases.

findings.summary: A010 assertion uses toContain('## Build Status') — confirms heading exists but not that content follows the spec mockup format
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: toContain('## Build Status') at worktree.test.ts:350 verifies the section exists in the worktree context file — matching A010's scope. The content under the heading (success/failure messages) is tested separately in the A004/A005/A006 test at line 353 which covers the build-failure scenario. Heading assertion + content assertion across two tests provides full coverage.

findings.summary: A008 uses toBeDefined() for depsInstalled — weaker than contract's 'exists' intent, passes even if value is false
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: A008 contract says depsInstalled 'exists' — toBeDefined() at worktree.test.ts:392 literally means 'exists and is not undefined.' depsInstalled is typed boolean (worktree.ts:28) — it's always true or false, never undefined. The assertion matches the contract's intent exactly. In this fixture (no lockfile), the value is deterministically false, which toBeDefined() accepts correctly.

findings.summary: Spec AC4 says warning includes 'the failed command' but work.ts outputs generic message without the actual command string
findings.closed_reason: upstream

build_concerns.summary: getBuildCommandString reads ana.json a second time during worktree creation

entries.scope_summary: `ana work complete --merge --json <slug>` produces invalid JSON output. Progress messages ("Merging PR...", "PR merged.") and pull-recovery warnings write to stdout before the JSON envelope, breaking any consumer that pipes the output to `jq` or parses it programmatically. The fix guards these messages behind `if (!options?.json)` and adds test coverage for the `--merge --json` interaction.

findings.summary: A004 meta assertion uses toBeTypeOf('object') — passes for null
findings.closed_reason: Structural type check is correct: toBeTypeOf('object') at work-merge.test.ts:388 verifies meta field exists and is an object. computeChainHealth (proofSummary.ts:1338) never returns null — it always returns a ChainHealth object. The typeof null gotcha is theoretical. Specific meta field assertions exist in the main work.test.ts health tests. Same pattern closed for audit-json-severity-summary-C3 this session.

findings.summary: A009/A010 satisfied by existing tests but lack @ana tags for this contract
findings.closed_reason: Missing @ana tags pattern: A009/A010 from fix-merge-json-pollution contract are satisfied by existing tests in work-merge.test.ts but lack tags for this specific contract. Same systemic pattern documented and closed 10+ times this session. Verify accepted it — behavior IS covered, tagging is cosmetic.

entries.scope_summary: The CLI has no concept of committing infrastructure. Init writes files to disk. Setup enriches them. Neither commits. A teammate who clones gets nothing Anatomia-related. The investment of a 10-minute setup session exists only on one developer's machine. This scope adds `ana init commit` — a command that commits Anatomia infrastructure files to the artifact branch, distinct from pipeline artifacts (`ana artifact save`) and proof chain data (`ana work complete`, `ana proof close/promote/strengthen`).

findings.summary: Push failure test doesn't test push failure — tests push skip (no remote)
No integration test for pull conflict abort path
No integration test for worktree guard — verified by source inspection only
findings.closed_reason: Covered by pattern: init commit worktree guard at commit.ts:287-290 is the identical pattern (isWorktreeDirectory → console.error → process.exit(1)) being tested by the worktree-guard-tests scope for init, setup complete, and work complete. Same function, same guard shape. The scope could include init commit as a one-test extension — but the detection function is unit-tested and the guard pattern is integration-tested for 3 other commands.

findings.summary: discoverDirtyFiles returns empty array on git status failure without reporting why
findings.closed_reason: Intentional defensive behavior: discoverDirtyFiles at commit.ts:102-103 returns empty array when git status fails — 'assume no dirty files' is the safe default. The caller skips the commit step ('Everything is up to date'). This is misleading on git failure but not dangerous — no data corruption, user would notice files aren't committed and re-run. git status failure requires corrupted repo or missing git, both exceptional conditions.

findings.summary: A013 (--no-verify) has no runtime assertion — test name claims it but only verifies commit happened
findings.closed_reason: Testing git flag behavior: --no-verify at commit.ts:330 is a git flag. The test at commit.test.ts:349 proves the commit succeeds. Verifying that --no-verify actually bypasses hooks would test git's behavior, not our code. To assert the flag is passed, you'd need to mock spawnSync and check args — invasive for a hardcoded flag. The flag is visible by source inspection at line 330 and documented in the header comment at line 16.

findings.summary: Still present — see worktree-isolation proof finding: guard commands lack integration tests
findings.closed_reason: upstream

runGit trims stdout which corrupts git status --porcelain first line — worked around with direct spawnSync

assertions.says: Running init from a worktree exits with an error
The init worktree error tells the user to use the main directory

Running work complete from a worktree exits with an error
The work complete worktree error tells the user to use the main directory

entries.scope_summary: Three CLI commands hard-block execution when run from inside a worktree: `init`, `setup complete`, and `work complete`. A fourth (`scan --save`) warns but continues. These guards prevent repo corruption — running `init` from a worktree creates a nested `.ana/`, running `work complete` corrupts the proof chain. All four guards are verified only by source inspection (A028, A030, A031, A032 in the worktree-isolation verify report). No integration test verifies that the guard actually fires when a command runs from inside a worktree. This scope adds those tests.

findings.summary: A009 test only verifies cwd restoration, not process.exit or console spy cleanup
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in fe728e70 — A009 test at line 149 now verifies console.error and console.warn restoration (expect(console.error).toBe(originalError), expect(console.warn).toBe(originalWarn)) in addition to cwd restoration. The afterEach cleanup guarantees all three are restored.

findings.summary: Console and process.exit spies restored inline after assertions — leak on assertion failure
findings.closed_reason: Fixed in fe728e70 — moved spy restoration from inline (after assertions) to afterEach (guaranteed cleanup). console.error, console.warn, and process.exit spies are saved in beforeEach and restored in afterEach. If an assertion fails, afterEach still runs — no spy leak into subsequent tests.

findings.summary: A009 test is order-dependent — relies on running after the other describe blocks
findings.closed_reason: Acceptable: vitest runs tests within a file sequentially (guaranteed). The A009 test at line 149 verifies afterEach cleanup by checking cwd restoration. It's order-dependent by design — it must run after the guard tests to verify their cleanup. Vitest's sequential execution within files makes this reliable. The test name and comment (line 151-153) document the dependency.

build_concerns.summary: Pre-existing lint warning in git-operations.ts:198 (unused eslint-disable)

entries.scope_summary: Two proof findings (proof-health-v2-C12, proof-health-v2-C8) identify untested defensive branches in work.ts. The UNKNOWN verification result warning and the git pull conflict error path have zero test coverage. Both protect users from silent failures — a malformed verify report proceeding unnoticed, and a rebase conflict during completion losing work. The user wants regression tests for both paths.

findings.summary: exitCalls count assertion uses toBeGreaterThan(0) instead of toBe(1)
findings.closed_reason: Adequate: toBeGreaterThan(0) at work.test.ts:3364 proves process.exit was called at least once. The test verifies the guard fires — exact call count (toBe(1)) adds marginal value since the guard calls exit once and throws, preventing a second call. Same weak-assertion pattern closed throughout this session.

findings.summary: Conflict test creates bareDir and cloneDir as siblings of tempDir — afterEach only cleans tempDir, so these directories leak on each test run
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-ci-matrix-and-broken-tests: original conflict test with bareDir/cloneDir as siblings of tempDir (path.join(tempDir, '..')) removed from work.test.ts. Replaced by mocked version in work-ci-mocked.test.ts that creates no temp directories outside tempDir. Zero matches for bareDir/cloneDir in work.test.ts. Verify upstream finding C4 confirmed.

findings.summary: UNKNOWN test mocks process.exit as no-op, causing execution to pass through the early UNKNOWN guard at work.ts:1513 before reaching the target branch at work.ts:870 — exercises two exit points instead of isolating one
findings.closed_reason: Accepted: test comment at work.test.ts:3284-3286 documents the dual exit-point coupling explicitly. process.exit mock is necessary because work.ts has two UNKNOWN exit paths (line 1513 and line 870). Test exercises both intentionally. Fixing requires restructuring UNKNOWN handling in work.ts — marginal gain for scope-level effort.

findings.summary: A003 assertion uses toBeDefined() for proof chain entry — matches contract 'exists' matcher but weaker than testing standards preference for specific value assertions
findings.closed_reason: Contract-aligned: toBeDefined() at work.test.ts:3308 matches the contract's 'exists' matcher. The assertion proves the proof chain entry was created. Specific field assertions exist in other tests (result, contract counts, timing). Same structural assertion pattern closed throughout this session.

findings.summary: Two different result parsers with different casing: getVerifyResult returns 'unknown' (lowercase), parseResult in proofSummary returns 'UNKNOWN' (uppercase) — works correctly but fragile coupling between two parallel implementations

findings.closed_reason: Harmless CSS redundancy: cursor:pointer on parent div (line 111) and inner span (line 125). Inner inherits from parent. Removing the inner one changes nothing visually. Belt-and-suspenders styling.

findings.summary: Toggle pattern deviates from AssertionLedger — uses !expanded toggle vs separate true/false calls
findings.closed_reason: Design choice: !expanded toggle at FindingsList.tsx:104 is simpler than AssertionLedger's separate setExpanded(true)/setExpanded(false) calls. Both produce identical behavior. The single toggle handler is a simplification, not a deviation.

The CLI and agent template should surface these URLs at the right moments: orientation points where users are deciding what to do, not flow moments where they're in the middle of doing something.

entries.scope_summary: Node 20 passed its maintenance EOL in April 2026 — it no longer receives security patches. Our engines field still declares `>=20`, our CI still tests against it, and our docs still advertise it. Meanwhile Node 22 is current LTS (maintained until April 2027) and Node 24 is Current (LTS in October 2026). We should stop validating against a dead version and start validating against the version our users will be on next.

entries.scope_summary: The website prebuild script (`website/scripts/extract-docs-data.ts`) has a hidden side effect: `updateDynamicMdxValues()` rewrites tracked MDX source files, replacing `{/* ana:dynamic ... */}` markers with computed values. This causes silent dirty state on every build, guaranteed rebase conflicts on docs-touching branches, and maintains a redundant data path — every value hardcoded into MDX source already exists as a runtime function in `website/lib/docs-data/`. Separately, three generated files in `public/` (llms.txt, llms-full.txt, search-index.json) are tracked in git despite being regenerated by every prebuild run, compounding the same rebase-conflict problem.

Milliseconds total. No runtime cost — the HTML is pre-rendered.

These observations enter the proof chain as upstream findings with `status: 'lesson'`. But the original finding stays active because nothing connects the upstream claim to the original. Learn must manually read the upstream finding's prose, identify which original it references, verify the claim, and close it with `ana proof close`.

entries.scope_summary: The lesson status occupies a semantic niche already filled by close-with-reason. Post-availability adoption data shows 128 findings closed with "accept/intentional" reasons vs. 6 using the lesson command — the workflow voted with its feet. The Learn agent's triage phases produce close, keep, or promote — lesson isn't part of the loop. No agent reads lessons, no command surfaces them, and Ana's own instruction to "surface relevant lessons" via `proof context` is broken because `getProofContext` filters lessons out. Meanwhile, 116 upstream auto-lessons (pipeline forensics about contract quality and tag collisions) dilute the concept so thoroughly that a lesson query would return 95% noise. The status adds a fourth value every consumer must handle while contributing no retrievable information.

entries.scope_summary: Prevent concurrent pipeline sessions from corrupting each other. Learn Session 5 uncovered a real failure: two AnaVerify sessions ran against the same scope simultaneously. Session #2 archived a valid verify report, created a duplicate PR, force-pushed over the merged branch, and left `work complete` unable to detect the merge. Recovery required git internals knowledge our target customer doesn't have. The pipeline assumes one agent per stage but enforces nothing — this scope adds mechanical enforcement.

findings.summary: checkConcurrencyGuard has dead `force` parameter — never passed true from production call sites
isTimestampRecent duplicates checkConcurrencyGuard logic — both parse .saves.json, extract timestamp, compare against CONCURRENCY_TIMEOUT_MS

No boundary test at exactly 1-hour timeout — tests use 2-hour-old (stale) and new Date() (fresh), missing 59m59s and 60m01s cases
plan_started_at written with force:true — spec only mentions verify_started_at force-write, this is over-building for consistency
findings.closed_reason: Correct engineering, not over-building: plan_started_at force-write is necessary for the concurrency guard. Without force-write, a crashed plan session's old timestamp persists (write-once prevents overwrite on re-entry) and blocks the next session. The guard checks recency — it needs the LATEST timestamp, not the first. Same reasoning as verify_started_at force-write at line 1797.

findings.summary: A019 mock returns raw 'MERGED\n' string but implementation uses -q '.[0].state' jq filter — mock bypasses JSON parsing path
findings.closed_reason: Mock is correct: gh pr list --json state -q '.[0].state' outputs raw string MERGED (jq extracts from JSON inside gh). Mock returning 'MERGED\n' matches actual gh output. Implementation at work.ts:1525 compares trimmed stdout against 'MERGED'. The JSON parsing happens inside gh, not in our code — the mock correctly simulates the CLI's actual behavior.

entries.scope_summary: The scan engine captures local git branches alongside remote branches via `git branch -a`. When scan.json is committed and shared (via `ana init commit`, AnaDocs, or agent consumption), developer-local branch state — stale refs, experiment branches, old sprint fossils — becomes permanent project intelligence. Two developers scanning the same repo produce different branch lists. This was discovered when our own scan.json was committed with 98 branches, 89 of which were stale local refs.

daysSinceScan uses Math.floor — scan exactly 7.9 days old shows as 7, not stale
findings.closed_reason: Correct and consistent: Math.floor at scan-freshness.ts:56 produces daysSinceScan. The staleness check at line 89 uses daysSinceScan > 7 (strictly greater). A 7.9-day-old scan floors to 7, 7 > 7 is false, shows 'current' — correct because it hasn't completed 8 full days. Display and comparison use the same floored value, so the number shown matches the threshold logic. Boundary is deterministic.

entries.scope_summary: The scanner produces incorrect output in two narrow, high-impact ways: it flags template URL patterns as critical secrets (false positives), and it generates unrunnable `npm vitest run` commands for monorepo projects. Both erode first-user trust — the tool's own output is untrustworthy. A user who sees a false CRITICAL finding or a broken test command in their first scan has no reason to trust anything else the tool says.

entries.scope_summary: The scan engine produces no application shape and no AI SDK detection for non-Node projects. A Python FastAPI project gets `shape: 'unknown'` and `aiSdk: null` even when the framework and AI dependencies are correctly detected. Two independent gaps, same disease: the engine detects non-Node signals but discards or never routes them to shape and AI SDK classification.

findings.closed_reason: Source inspection is valid verification per Rule 2 (enforcement tests for structural invariants). Verify classified as accept. Same pattern as 5+ findings closed this session (worktree-isolation-C1, phase-timing-fix-C4, etc.) — tag collision makes per-scope tag tracking ambiguous but the behavior IS verified.

findings.summary: Module JSDoc says 'classifies Node projects' but function now also classifies non-Node
findings.closed_reason: Fixed: module JSDoc at applicationShape.ts:2 updated from 'classifies Node projects' to 'classifies projects'. Function now handles Python, Go, and Rust via FRAMEWORK_TO_SHAPE lookup (lines 127-143) added by this scope. One word change, zero behavior impact. Commit 105875de.

findings.summary: FRAMEWORK_TO_SHAPE map placed between JSDoc and function — unconventional positioning
findings.closed_reason: Code style — deliberate locality: FRAMEWORK_TO_SHAPE (lines 125-143) is placed between JSDoc and function because it's the function's only constant, used exclusively at line 148. Co-location with its sole consumer is a valid organizational choice. No behavioral impact, no confusion risk.

findings.summary: A001-A007 multi-tagged on single describe block — assertion-to-test tracing is ambiguous
findings.closed_reason: Multi-tag is a summary annotation: line 350 tags the describe block with all 7 IDs as a group marker. Individual tests within the block have their own specific tags (A001 at line 309, A002 at 294, A003 at 284, A005 at 107, A006 at 120, A007 at 143). Tracing works at the individual test level — the describe-level tag is supplementary.

entries.scope_summary: `detectProjectType` returns `'node'` the moment `package.json` exists. It never checks whether pyproject.toml also exists. Result: litellm (47k stars, YC W23, Python FastAPI) and langflow (140k stars, Python FastAPI) detect as "Node.js" because they have `package.json` + `package-lock.json` at root for their frontend UI tooling. A potential pilot customer seeing "Language: Node.js" on their Python project is an immediate credibility failure for Anatomia's core value proposition.

findings.summary: Tier 4 no-lockfile + pyproject with no real deps returns 0.70 — same confidence as Tier 5 bare package.json, indistinguishable to downstream consumers
findings.closed_reason: Intentional: Tier 4 pyproject with no real deps falls through to Tier 5 bare package.json — both return node at 0.70. The equal confidence is correct: both situations represent equally uncertain Node detection. If pyproject.toml has no real Python deps, it's not a Python project. The confidence communicates uncertainty, not provenance.

findings.summary: A012 frameworkDeps test verifies detector-level cascade but not the actual scan-engine.ts ternary conditional — the ternary fix is tested structurally, not behaviorally
nextSection search uses indexOf('\n[') which misses a section header at position 0 of the sliced block (no preceding newline)
findings.closed_reason: Self-correcting edge case — indexOf('\n[') at projectType.ts:84 and :110 (hasPythonProjectDeps) could miss a section header at position 0 of the sliced block, but that only occurs when the matched section is empty (next header immediately follows). Empty section means no deps found, so the function correctly returns false. Same pattern and reasoning as fix-polyglot-rust-ts-ruby-C2 closed this session.

findings.summary: Spec says Tier 2 workspaces is 0.90, Tier 1 lockfile-only is 0.95 — a monorepo with a lockfile gets LOWER confidence than a non-monorepo with a lockfile, which is counterintuitive
findings.closed_reason: upstream

entries.scope_summary: `detectProjectType` only checks for `pyproject.toml` as a competing manifest when `package.json` exists. It never checks for `Cargo.toml` (Rust) or `go.mod` (Go). Result: tabby (92.9% Rust, 20-crate Cargo workspace, 37k stars) is classified as TypeScript because it has a thin `package.json` for turborepo orchestration. This is the same disease class as the Python polyglot fix — a non-Node project with a secondary package.json for frontend/build tooling gets misclassified as Node.

findings.summary: Preflight .git detection regression — fileExists returns false for directories
findings.closed_reason: Not a regression: .git is checked with raw fs.stat at preflight.ts:94, NOT fileExists. fs.stat succeeds for both files and directories. The fileIndicators array (line 90) only contains package.json, go.mod, Cargo.toml, pyproject.toml — all files. Comment at lines 88-89 explicitly documents why .git is separate: 'it can be a directory (normal repos) or a file (worktrees/submodules), so neither fileExists nor dirExists covers both.'

findings.summary: Over-building — init/state.ts, init/index.ts, scan.ts changes unrelated to polyglot detection
findings.closed_reason: Historical build discipline observation: init/state.ts, init/index.ts, scan.ts changes included in the polyglot feature branch. The changes are correct regardless of which branch introduced them. Same pattern as unified-staleness-awareness-C2 — about commit history, not current code state. Cannot resurface.

findings.summary: A017 frameworkDeps test uses toBeDefined — passes even if framework is null
findings.closed_reason: Contract-aligned per Rule 3 (testing-standards): A017 contract uses matcher 'exists'. Test uses toBeDefined() — exact match. The closed upstream finding C6 from this same proof confirms: 'A017 contract matcher exists permits weak test.' The weakness is in the contract's precision, not the test's assertion.

findings.summary: hasRustWorkspace catch block unreachable — regex cannot throw
findings.closed_reason: Harmless defensive code — hasRustWorkspace at projectType.ts:34-39 wraps regex.test() in try/catch. RegExp.prototype.test() with a static regex and string input cannot throw. The catch block is unreachable but does no harm. Consistent with project's defensive coding style in detector functions.

findings.summary: Priority ordering Python > Rust > Go in Tier 3 is implicit and untested
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-polyglot-detection-hygiene scope — priority ordering test added at polyglot.test.ts:615. Creates project with all four competing manifests (pyproject.toml + Cargo.toml + Gemfile + go.mod) and asserts Python wins. Second test at line 641 removes Python and asserts Rust wins. Cascade order is now regression-tested.

entries.scope_summary: Learn's orientation phase requires 3+ commands and manual synthesis to understand the shape of the proof chain before triage. The severity × action cross-tab (the intersection of these two dimensions) is the single most useful orientation data — it tells Learn the shape of work, not just counts. "4 risk" is less actionable than "3 risk/scope + 1 risk/monitor." Additionally, Learn needs to know what shipped recently and how many findings are stale. Today this requires running `ana proof audit`, `ana proof stale`, and manually checking the last 3 entries.

entries.scope_summary: Learn starts every session from zero. With 55+ active findings, there's no way to distinguish "already triaged" from "appeared since last time." The session wastes time re-reading known findings and risks missing new ones in the noise. The developer wants Learn to know what's new — and filter to just those findings.

findings.summary: commitAndPushProofChanges and pullBeforeRead exported from proof.ts instead of extracted to git-operations.ts
Duplicated learn state reading logic — matrix block and filter block both parse state.json independently
findings.closed_reason: Not duplication: matrix block (line 1717) and filter block (line 1913) are mutually exclusive code paths. Matrix early-returns before the filter section runs. They never both parse state.json in the same invocation. Extracting to a shared read would parse the file even when neither --new nor --matrix is used.

findings.summary: --new and --since can be provided simultaneously — --since silently wins with no documentation
findings.closed_reason: Intentional precedence: --since is checked first (proof.ts:1902), --new is in the else block (line 1910). Explicit date overrides implicit 'since last session.' Standard CLI pattern — more specific flag wins. No ambiguity, no data loss. Behavior is correct and unsurprising.

findings.summary: No @ana tags for A025-A029 — template/doc assertions verified by source inspection only
findings.closed_reason: Rule 2 (enforcement tests): A025-A029 are template/documentation content assertions with matcher 'contains' on template_content, readme_content, guide_content, toolbelt_content. Source-content verification is the correct method for structural invariants. Same pattern as 8+ findings closed this session under Rule 2.

findings.summary: learn.ts uses synchronous fs (existsSync, readFileSync, writeFileSync, mkdirSync) while init assets.ts uses async fs
findings.closed_reason: Consistent with commands layer patterns: proof.ts has 37 sync fs calls (existsSync, readFileSync, writeFileSync). learn.ts follows the same pattern. Init uses async because it runs parallel scan I/O — different use case. Sync fs is standard for single-shot CLI commands reading one small file.

entries.scope_summary: Re-init runs a fresh scan but then discards the scan's metadata fields (`name`, `language`, `framework`, `packageManager`) in favor of potentially stale values from the old config. A project that changed its primary language, switched package managers, or renamed itself gets stale ana.json fields even after re-init. The user wants these four fields to refresh automatically, the same way `anaVersion` and `lastScanAt` already do.

entries.scope_summary: There is no single command that answers "is my Anatomia installation healthy?" The health data exists — scan freshness, context maturity, skill enrichment, proof chain trajectory, CLI version, stale work items — but it's scattered across `setup check`, `work status`, `proof health`, and `update-check.ts`. Nobody runs all four. A customer who just finished init has no way to know what's healthy, what's next, and what needs attention.

findings.summary: A001-A005 tests verify data model, not terminal output — contract targets output.lines
findings.closed_reason: Valid testing strategy: tests verify the assessment data model (pass/fail/warn status) rather than terminal output strings. The data model IS what produces the output — if the model is correct, the output is correct. Model-level testing is more stable than string-matching formatted terminal output, which is brittle to formatting changes.

entries.feature: Fix Deep Tier Sampling & Finding Accuracy

assertions.says: The validation rule finds all API routes in a project, not just sampled ones

Aliases with hash prefix are recognized as path aliases

Deep files get sampled even when the budget is smaller than the total file count
The default sample budget is 750 files

The tree-sitter parse cost comment reflects actual measured performance

entries.scope_summary: The deep tier's proportional sampler produces a depth-biased sample that systematically over-represents shallow files (config, types, barrel exports) and under-represents application code (API routes, services, domain logic). This bias causes the API validation finding to produce misleading results on real customer repos — "1/1 sampled API routes have no input validation" on Dub (which has 139 API routes, 76 with validation imports), "12/12" on Formbricks (which has 96 routes, 30+ with direct validation). The error-boundaries finding has the same sample-dependency problem. And the import convention classifier has a separate bug that misclassifies alias imports, producing wrong results for any project with multiple tsconfig path aliases.

findings.summary: VALIDATION_PATH_PATTERNS check can false-positive on non-validation imports containing 'schema' or 'validate'
allocateBudget can return total exceeding budget when budget < non-empty bucket count
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-sampler-budget-overflow scope — remaining > 0 guard added at proportionalSampler.ts:81. Verified: first-pass loop now stops when budget is exhausted. Buckets ordered [shallow, mid, deep] so small budgets favor shallower files intentionally.

findings.summary: classifyTSImport line 83 replace('/*', '') is dead code for new alias format
findings.closed_reason: Not dead code — replace('/*', '') at imports.ts:83 strips glob suffix from tsconfig path aliases (e.g., '@/*' → '@/'). For new-format aliases without '/*', the replace is a no-op that returns the string unchanged. Backward-compatible handling of both alias formats.

findings.summary: No test exercises VALIDATION_PATH_PATTERNS false positive boundary (e.g., import containing 'schema' in a non-validation context)
Validation rule reads all route files synchronously via readFileSync — established pattern (matches secrets.ts) but could be noticeable at 500+ routes
A021 has no @ana tag in new test files — verified by source inspection only (comment-only change, appropriate)
findings.closed_reason: upstream

Pre-existing lint warning in git-operations.ts:198 (unused eslint-disable)

entries.scope_summary: The polyglot language detector has two gaps that produce trust-killing misclassifications on real customer repos:

findings.summary: Stale docstring — says 'Python → Go → Rust → Ruby → PHP' but polyglot tier order is Python → Rust → Ruby → Go
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-polyglot-detection-hygiene scope — docstring at projectType.ts:133 now documents both orders: polyglot tier (Python → Rust → Ruby → Go) and non-package.json fallthrough (Python → Go → Rust → Ruby → PHP), with explanation that the difference is intentional (content checks vs existence checks).

findings.summary: indexOf('\n[') section boundary misses header at position 0 of sliced block — inherited from hasPythonProjectDeps pattern
findings.closed_reason: Self-correcting edge case — indexOf('\n[') at projectType.ts:60 could miss a section header at position 0 of the sliced block, but that only occurs when the [workspace.dependencies] section is empty (next header immediately follows). Empty section means no tauri dep, so the function correctly returns false. The imprecision produces the right result.

findings.summary: Tauri discriminator omits Cargo.toml from indicators — downstream consumers can't tell Rust is present
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-polyglot-detection-hygiene scope — indicators.push('Cargo.toml') added before pnpm-workspace.yaml push in both Tauri paths: Tier 3 at projectType.ts:207 and Tier 4 at projectType.ts:257. Verified both lines present.

findings.summary: Ruby detection is existence-only — no Gemfile content analysis, so a Gemfile with only dev gems still triggers Ruby
Tag collision — @ana IDs A001-A019 used by both old contracts and this contract in same file, creating ambiguity for tooling
findings.closed_reason: Systemic tag collision — contract IDs A001-A019 reused across multiple contracts in polyglot.test.ts. Same systemic pattern documented across the proof chain. Tags are cosmetic identifiers; the tests themselves are correct and independent.

findings.summary: No test for Gemfile + Cargo.toml [workspace] coexistence — Rust wins by code position but this priority is untested
findings.closed_reason: upstream

findings.summary: hasTauriWorkspaceDep catch block unreachable — regex cannot throw, same pattern as rust-go-polyglot-detection-C4
findings.closed_reason: Harmless defensive code: hasTauriWorkspaceDep catch block wraps a regex .test() call which cannot throw. Same pattern as rust-go-polyglot-detection-C4 (hasRustWorkspace). Two lines of unreachable code, zero behavioral impact. Not worth tracking as active finding.

build_concerns.summary: hasTauriWorkspaceDep catch block unreachable — regex cannot throw, same pattern as hasRustWorkspace
Pre-existing lint warning in git-operations.ts (unused eslint-disable directive) — not introduced by this build

entries.scope_summary: V2-Alpha testing across 20 real open-source repos revealed detection gaps in four areas: deployment platforms, CI systems, workspace tools, and Vercel AI provider packages. The core stack detection (language, framework, database, auth, testing) is strong. The gaps are in areas where the ecosystem moved in 2025-2026 and our detection maps haven't kept pace.

findings.summary: Workspace label tests verify a replicated helper, not the actual scan-engine.ts ternary
@openrouter/ai-sdk-provider added to AI_PACKAGES but not in spec — over-building with zero test coverage
findings.closed_reason: Valid data addition: @openrouter/ai-sdk-provider IS an AI SDK provider. Adding it to AI_PACKAGES is a list entry, not logic — the detection loop is tested, individual package names are ecosystem facts. Over-building relative to spec but the addition is correct. No new code path, no test needed for a data entry.

findings.summary: AI_SDK_EXCLUSIONS set recreated on every detectServiceDeps call — could be module-level constant
findings.closed_reason: Micro-optimization: AI_SDK_EXCLUSIONS Set created inside detectServiceDeps which runs once per scan. Scan takes seconds. Set construction takes nanoseconds. Moving to module-level saves no meaningful time. Verify classified as accept — correctly non-blocking.

findings.summary: Wildcard capitalization only tested with single-word providers — no test for hyphenated wildcard input like @ai-sdk/foo-bar

build_concerns.summary: Workspace label tests mirror scan-engine ternary indirectly — could diverge if ternary is refactored
Wildcard capitalization is naive for hyphenated provider names (e.g., some-provider → Some-provider)

entries.scope_summary: Non-Node projects with a package.json (Ruby/Mastodon, Rust/Biome) get JS commands in ana.json. The pipeline executes these mechanically — Build runs `yarn run test -- --run` on a Ruby project. The entire pipeline produces wrong results. Fix the scan engine and init layer so non-Node projects get correct native commands or honest nulls, and ensure the user-facing flow (init display + setup) guides non-Node users to configure any remaining null commands before entering the pipeline.

findings.summary: A020 has no tagged test — verified by source inspection only
findings.closed_reason: Rule 2 (enforcement tests): A020 verified by source inspection. Source-content verification is the correct method for structural invariants. Same pattern closed 10+ times this session.

findings.summary: AC11 (Skills Detected section) tests absence of something never present — skills manifest contains skill file names, not test commands
findings.closed_reason: upstream

findings.summary: displaySuccessMessage treats empty string test command as null for init display — consistent with upstream blank sanitizer
buildNonNodeCommands returns early per-language without fallthrough — adding a new language requires a new if-block, no extensibility pattern
findings.closed_reason: Appropriate for current scale: 5 languages with if-blocks (Python, Go, Rust, Ruby, PHP). Each block returns early with language-specific commands. Adding a language is one if-block. A lookup table or plugin system for 5 cases is over-engineering. Refactor if the list grows past 10. Verify classified as accept.

findings.summary: getBuildCommandString exported solely for testing with @internal tag — follows project convention but increases public API surface
findings.closed_reason: Rule 1 (exports-for-testing): getBuildCommandString is used internally within worktree.ts (lines 589, 592) and exported for test access (nonNodeCommands.test.ts:3). The export serves the test file — internal calls don't need it. The @internal tag explicitly documents this intent. TypeScript has no module-private exports.

findings.summary: worktree-build-step-C3 partially resolved — misleading fallback fixed but duplicate I/O pattern remains
findings.closed_reason: upstream

findings.summary: detectNonNodeTesting Ruby branch uses existsSync (synchronous) inside an async function — inconsistent with other branches that use async reads
findings.closed_reason: Functional, negligible impact: two existsSync calls at scan-engine.ts:92-93 inside an async function. Go branch uses async reads, Ruby branch uses sync checks — inconsistent but both work. Two file-existence checks take nanoseconds during a multi-second scan. The function is async because other branches need it, not because every branch must be. Verify classified as accept.

build_concerns.summary: AC10 (scoped JS commands for Rust monorepo) lacks direct test — covered by language guard
AC11 (Skills Detected section) and AC20 (persist after setup) require E2E test coverage

entries.scope_summary: The scan reads every workspace package's dependencies, framework hints, tsconfigs, and bin fields — then merges it all into flat root-level fields (`allDeps`, `stack.framework`, `stack.testing`) and loses the per-package identity. A customer with a Next.js frontend and an Express API backend sees one detected framework, one testing framework, one set of commands. The second surface is invisible.

findings.summary: Contract A017 value 'my-package' doesn't match test fixture normalization result 'my-packagejs'
findings.closed_reason: upstream

findings.summary: A023 tests STRONG_FRAMEWORK_CONFIGS presence as proxy for FRAMEWORK_HINTS count — FRAMEWORK_HINTS is not exported
findings.closed_reason: Valid proxy testing: FRAMEWORK_HINTS is not exported. Test uses STRONG_FRAMEWORK_CONFIGS (exported, related) as proxy to verify the detection data exists with correct shape. Alternative would be exporting FRAMEWORK_HINTS solely for test access — a valid choice per Rule 1, but the developer chose the proxy approach instead. Both are acceptable.

findings.summary: A021 tests data shape availability, not actual terminal output containing 'Surfaces' string
findings.closed_reason: Valid testing strategy: test verifies the data shape (assessment object with surface fields) rather than terminal output strings. The data model IS what produces the output — if the model is correct, the output is correct. Model-level testing is more stable than string-matching formatted terminal output. Same reasoning as add-doctor-command-C1.

findings.summary: nuxt and astro missing from FRAMEWORK_DISPLAY_NAMES — surfaces display lowercase keys instead of 'Nuxt'/'Astro'
findings.closed_reason: Fixed: nuxt and astro added to FRAMEWORK_DISPLAY_NAMES at displayNames.ts:20. Both entries present: nuxt: 'Nuxt', astro: 'Astro'. Surfaces with these frameworks now display proper-cased names.

findings.summary: deriveRawName @scope stripping handles segment-level scoped names but path-level scoped packages use last path segment after split, making the @scope branch in deriveRawName unreachable for standard monorepo layouts

Collision disambiguation can still produce duplicates if two version-like paths share the same parent (e.g., packages/api/v1 and packages/api/v2 both become api-v1 and api-v2 — fine, but apps/api/v1 and packages/api/v1 would both become api-v1 after version normalization)
29 FRAMEWORK_HINTS entries vs spec's claim of 18+9=27 — 2 entries existed before this build (react-router.config.ts, astro.config.ts), making the true delta 11 new entries not 9
findings.closed_reason: Historical spec arithmetic: spec said 18+9=27 entries but 2 entries (react-router.config.ts, astro.config.ts) existed before this build. True delta is 11 not 9, total is 29 not 27. All 29 entries are correct — every framework hint is valid. The code is right, the spec undercounted. Cannot recur.

EnrichedPackage type change required updating 7 test files with default field values

entries.scope_summary: Make surface awareness real end-to-end. The scan already detects surfaces (shipped, validated against 12 repos). This scope makes the system USE that intelligence: ana.json stores per-surface commands, the pipeline targets the right surface, and the proof chain records which surface each run verified. A developer scoping website work gets website test commands. A developer scoping CLI work gets CLI test commands. Cross-surface work falls back to root commands.

findings.summary: deriveSurface logic duplicated in test — test reimplements work.ts logic instead of importing it
Surface path injected into shell command without sanitization — paths with spaces or special chars produce broken subshell
findings.closed_reason: Known pkg.path sanitization family — surface.path injected into shell command with single quotes at state.ts:512/519/522/528. Same disease as monorepo-build-scoping-C5 which tracks the root cause. All instances share the fix: proper shell escaping for paths with spaces or special characters.

findings.summary: Non-Node surface gets empty commands object instead of null commands — no native command generation for Rust/Go surfaces
A019 scope rejection test depends on cwd-based findProjectRoot — fragile coupling between test isolation and global state
findings.closed_reason: Established CLI testing pattern — process.chdir with try/finally guard at scope-surface-validation.test.ts:100-108. Same pattern used throughout work.test.ts, init.test.ts, and other CLI tests. The finally block mitigates cwd pollution risk. Accepted as the codebase's standard approach to testing cwd-dependent CLI behavior.

findings.summary: pkg.path injection without sanitization — pre-existing across monorepo-build-scoping-C5, flip-monorepo-commands-C4, and now surface generation
findings.closed_reason: upstream

findings.summary: displaySuccessMessage surface padding hardcoded at padEnd(9) — surface names longer than 8 chars will misalign
findings.closed_reason: Cosmetic: padEnd(9) at state.ts:1023 handles typical surface names (cli, website, api — all under 8 chars). Longer names misalign the display column but don't break functionality. If misalignment occurs, it's visible and fixable with one line (dynamic max-length). Verify classified as accept.

findings.summary: config delete on top-level machine-managed fields (anaVersion, name, etc.) blocked by MACHINE_MANAGED_FIELDS guard, but delete on whole 'surfaces' key is allowed — could wipe all surfaces
A028 Zod schema test — malformed entry defaults checked but no test for completely invalid surfaces value (e.g., surfaces: 42)
findings.closed_reason: Tests Zod internals: z.array(SurfaceSchema) mechanically rejects non-array inputs (surfaces: 42 → Zod type error). Testing this verifies Zod's .array() type guard, not our schema definition. The tests DO cover malformed array entries (wrong shape, missing fields) — that's where our Zod schema matters. Verify classified as accept.

build_concerns.summary: Surface path injected into shell commands without sanitization — inherits pre-existing pkg.path risk
Surface derivation logic duplicated between work.ts and test file — could drift silently

entries.scope_summary: Make surface awareness visible and queryable beyond the pipeline. Stages 1 and 2 shipped surface detection (scan) and pipeline integration (ana.json schema, scope/plan/build/verify surface flow, proof chain surface field). But the rest of the system — proof chain queries, the quality dashboard, scaffold generators, diagnostics, and the Learn agent — doesn't expose surface knowledge yet. The system can USE surfaces but can't SHOW or QUERY them outside the pipeline flow.

findings.summary: A021 idempotency test checks pure function determinism, not backfill loop guard
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by fix-test-behavioral-coverage scope — trivial pure-function idempotency test replaced with behavioral backfill guard test at work.test.ts:6016. New test creates an entry with surface: 'website' and verifies the backfill doesn't overwrite it. A021 tag moved to the meaningful test.

findings.summary: assessSurfaces reads ana.json independently — adds to known duplicate-read pattern (add-doctor-command-C3)
findings.closed_reason: Known pattern: ana.json duplicate reads already tracked by add-doctor-command-C3 (debt/scope, kept active). This finding adds another instance (assessSurfaces) of the same disease. The pattern is documented and the fix is the same — pass parsed config as parameter. Verify classified as accept.

findings.summary: Redundant type annotation: surface?: string | undefined — the ? already implies | undefined
findings.closed_reason: TypeScript style nit: surface?: string already implies string | undefined. The explicit | undefined is redundant — same type signature either way. Zero behavioral, compilation, or runtime difference. Verify classified as accept.

findings.summary: Backfill iterates all chain.entries on every work complete — O(n) with no short-circuit after first fully-backfilled run
findings.closed_reason: Fixed by proof-migration-cleanup scope — backfill loop at work.ts:1099 now gated by !chain.migrations?.['surface_backfill']. After first work complete sets the migration marker at line 1180, the entire loop is permanently skipped. No longer O(n) on every work complete.

findings.summary: Surface validation reads ana.json from disk on every --surface invocation — consistent with existing patterns but adds to file I/O per command
findings.closed_reason: Consistent with established patterns: every proof command reads ana.json per invocation (branch checks, coAuthor, etc.). One additional readFileSync for surface validation follows the same convention. ana.json is a small file, read is nanoseconds. Verify classified as accept.

entries.scope_summary: Per-surface test commands in `createAnaJson` use direct runner invocation (`pnpm vitest run`) instead of the developer's actual test script (`pnpm run test`). This skips setup steps like `prisma:generate`, `dotenv`, `cross-env`, and build prerequisites. R3 pre-launch testing found 12 of 29 surfaces (41%) across 17 real repos produce mismatched commands; 8 would cause test failures on the first pipeline run. The fix: invert the priority so script passthrough comes first, with direct invocation as fallback for surfaces that lack a test script.

findings.summary: scripts['test'] !== undefined treats explicit null value as 'present' — a package.json with test: null would get script passthrough producing a broken pnpm run test

findings.closed_reason: Harmless over-building — Build updated makeTestCommand.test.ts assertions to match new behavior introduced by the surface test priority fix. Modifying existing test assertions to stay consistent with production code changes is standard practice, even when the file wasn't listed in contract file_changes.

findings.summary: A006 empty-string assertion uses toContain('run test') — weaker than other assertions that use toBe for exact match
findings.closed_reason: toContain('run test') is appropriate here — the test verifies the command includes the script invocation. Using toBe for exact match would couple the test to package manager detection (pnpm vs npm vs yarn prefix). The weaker matcher is deliberate flexibility, not imprecision.

entries.scope_summary: The `allocateBudget` function in the proportional sampler can allocate more files than the budget allows. When the budget is smaller than the number of non-empty depth buckets, the first-pass floor allocation exceeds the budget and `remaining` goes negative. The function's contract says "budget" and it should mean budget. Source: Learn Session 7, finding `fix-deep-tier-sampling-C2`.

findings.summary: A001 says field ('never returns more than budget') doesn't match its matcher (not_equals 0 — just checks non-empty). Budget enforcement is actually verified by A002.

findings.closed_reason: Budget=1 test verifies the contract: exactly 1 file returned, count doesn't exceed budget. Which specific file is returned depends on glob ordering and bucket iteration — an implementation detail, not a behavioral guarantee. The test is correct for what it asserts.

entries.scope_summary: The application shape priority chain lets dependency-based signals (MCP SDK, LangChain, CLI deps like yargs/arg) override framework-based identity, producing wrong shapes for projects whose primary identity is determined by their framework. This is #2 priority from R5 comprehensive validation (70 repos tested). 8 repos misclassified: 6 false "cli", 2 false "mcp-server." This fix addresses 5 directly; the remaining 3 need upstream fixes in primary selection (Issue #3) and framework detection (Issue #8).

entries.scope_summary: The primary package heuristic picks the package with the most files when no `apps/` directory with framework evidence exists. For library and platform monorepos, the biggest package is often the admin dashboard, test suite, i18n data, or example app — not the core product. This cascades to wrong framework detection, wrong shape, wrong UI system, and wrong version display.

findings.summary: A007 tiebreaker test uses toContain('larger') — would pass if result were 'larger-thing' or any string containing 'larger'
findings.closed_reason: Controlled fixture — only two roots: packages/small and packages/larger. No string in the fixture contains 'larger' other than the intended path. toContain is unambiguous within this test data. Fragility requires simultaneous fixture change to introduce an ambiguous path.

findings.summary: A026 caller test doesn't verify actual call site — it only shows selectPrimary accepts 3 args. Caller verified by source inspection (census.ts:571).
findings.closed_reason: Per testing-standards Rule 2: source-content assertions are acceptable as enforcement tests for structural invariants. Test at census-primary.test.ts:378 documents and enforces the 3-arg selectPrimary contract. Call site verification at census.ts:571 is confirmed by source inspection comment in the test.

findings.summary: Tier 4 (scoped+self-named) matches any package where bare === scope, regardless of projectDirName. @strapi/strapi matches in any repo whose packages include it, not just 'strapi' directories.

parsePackageName is a private helper but has no guard for empty-string input — returns { scope: '', bare: '' }. Harmless because nameMatchCandidates filters null packageName, but empty-string packageName would pass the filter and produce an empty bare match.
findings.closed_reason: Empty-string package name is invalid npm (npm publish rejects it). Callers filter null packageName before calling parsePackageName. Even if '' reached the function, bare: '' matches no directory name in the 4-tier scoring — empty string can't equal a real directory name.

findings.summary: No test for the Policy 1 + Policy 0 interaction: an apps/ package in a non-product path (e.g., 'examples/apps/web') — would Policy 0 filter it before Policy 1 can match?
IDENTITY_WORDS.has(bareLower) checks case-insensitively via bareLower, but the Set contains lowercase strings — this is correct but implicit. No comment documenting the case-insensitive intent.
findings.closed_reason: Variable name bareLower documents the case-insensitive intent — 'Lower' suffix indicates the value has been lowercased. The IDENTITY_WORDS Set containing lowercase strings is the natural complement. Adding a comment would be redundant with the naming convention.
